Ingka API Standards

Overview
> **Note**
> These standards are **inner source** and we welcome contributions from everyone in Ingka Group Digital.

This work stands at the heart of our digital foundation w.r.t APIs at Ingka. We want to enable creating "role model APIs" a no-brainer for the many teams at Ingka. This repository intends to hold what "good looks like" when it comes to all styles of APIs.

Table of contents
* API Styles
* API Reusability Classificatiobn
* Contributing
* Contact
* Licence

API Styles
An Application Programming Interface (API) is the general software construct that provides the ability to exchange data across software boundaries (such as components, systems and domains). This applies to both business and technology software. 

!image

The term "API" can be a broad brush and in the context of Ingka we mean the following styles:

Web API

- Synchronous integration based on API architecture styles such as REST Web API or Query Style Web API (e.g. GraphQL)
- Focus on supporting request-response integration related to operational use cases
- API URI and schema designed based on specific Data Concepts
- Uses Ingka & Inter level guidelines on URI path, API data concepts, naming standards and security
- Sometimes referred to in Ingka as "Business API", indicating an API that supports a specific task in the IKEA business model

You can find the standard for Web APIs here

Event API

- Event topics are published and subscribed to based on general Event streaming technologies
- Focus on asynchronous, message based integration across operational business processes
- Uses Ingka & Inter level guidelines on technology standards, event topic naming, payload schema and security


You can find the standard for Event APIs here

API Reusability Classification

API classified from the reusability perspective of how well an API is aligned with INGKA level policies, scope (boundary), standards and guidelines related to design of canonical, system independent APIs.
!reusability

Business API
A Business API is a reusable Published API that has a distinct capability within a corporate domain that can be functional or technical.

In practice a Business API

* exposes a core Ingka software capability that is reusable across the IKEA digital ecosystem
* is designed based on enterprise level capabilties and data representations with a longer lifetime than a specific implementation
* provides the surface of the Ingka Retail Platform that hides implementation and modernization complexity

System API

A System Specific API is a Published API that is based on System specific data models and design guidelines shared by a single or limited group of consumer systems.

Event-API

---

Introduction

Events are everywhere. Events are typically described differently by event producers. However, developers constantly have to learn how to consume events because there isn't a standardized way to specify events. This consequently reduces the potentiality of libraries, tooling, and platforms to facilitate the delivery, processing and tracing of events across involved systems. The Event-API Baseline is a specification for developing standard Event APIs to enable interoperability between services, platforms, and systems. It’s generic, so that it could be used for APIs that work over any protocol (e.g. SMF, MQTT, AMQP, gRPC, REST etc). The Event API baseline is applicable to everything that can implement an Event-Driven Architecture and send or receive events.

In case guidelines are changing, the following rules apply:

- Existing APIs are not required to be modified, but it is recommended to do so. Try to resolve all non-breaking changes based on this baseline as soon as new events to be publish.
- Clients of existing APIs must cope with APIs based on outdated rules.
- New APIs have to respect the current guidelines.

Notations and Terminology

Notational Conventions

The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in RFC 2119.

For clarity, when a component is marked as "OPTIONAL" this means that it is OPTIONAL for both the Producer and Consumer of an Event. In other words, a producer can choose to include that in an Event if it wants, and a consumer can choose to support that component if it wants. If a consumer does not support that feature, it is allowed to take any action it wants, including taking no action or generating an error, as long as it does not break any other requirements described by this specification.
However, the RECOMMENDED action is to ignore it. The producer SHOULD be prepared for the situation where a consumer ignores that feature.

While going through the documentation pay attention to the symbols below.

- 📚 The document is organized in two folds information manner.

This page contains highlights of different aspects of Event API and if you need more explanation then follow the read more page.
- 🚦 Validation would be enforced during publish API Specification.

- 📒 Platform specific rules.

Terminology

This specification defines the following terms:

Application

An "application" is any kind of digital product or computer program or a group of them. It MUST be a Producer, a Consumer or both. An application MAY be a microservice, serverless program, IoT device (sensor), etc. An application MAY be written in any number of different programming languages as long as they support the selected protocol. An application MUST also use a protocol supported by the messaging broker in order to connect and exchange events. Application as digital product MUST be registered in INGKA System Master Catalogue

Protocol

A protocol is the mechanism (wireline protocol OR API) by which events are exchanged between the application and the broker. Example protocol include, but are not limited to, AMQP, MQTT, JMS, REST, WebSocket.

Producer

The "producer" is a specific instance, process or device that creates the data structure describing the Event.

Consumer

A "consumer" receives the event and acts upon it. It uses the attributes and data to execute some logic, which MAY lead to the occurrence of new events.

Message

Events are transported from a source to a destination via messages. A message MUST contain a `payload` and MAY also contain `headers`. The headers MAY be subdivided into protocol-defined headers and header properties defined by the application which can act as supporting metadata. The payload contains the data, defined by the application, which MUST be serialized into a format (JSON, XML, Avro, binary, etc.). Since a message is a generic mechanism, it can support multiple interaction patterns such as **event, command, query**.

Event

Events are occurrences of state changes. Interested consumers (the destination) can subscribe to and react to events that are published from a producer (the source). An event will not identify a specific routing destination. The producer who published the events typically would have no idea what happened to it and receives no confirmation that it was processed. Events will contain two types of information: the Event Data representing the occurrence of event and context metadata providing contextual information about the occurrence, also known as message header. Context metadata will be encapsulated in the  Attributes, also known as message header. A single event occurrence MAY result in more than one event.

- Events are notifications to a status change.
- Events typically represent a change in the status of something that is important to the business.
- Events are records of what has occurred.
- Events are immutable, which means they cannot be altered. (Things that happened in the past cannot be changed.)

Payload

This includes information about the event i.e. details about the data that was changed, or more. See the Event Data section for more information.

Message Format

A message Format specifies how to serialize an event as a sequence of bytes. Stand-alone message formats, such as the JSON format, specify serialization independent of any protocol or storage medium. Protocol Bindings MAY define formats that are dependent on the protocol.

Attributes

Metadata of event will be encapsulated in the Attributes. Tools and application code can use this information to identify the relationship of Events to aspects of the system or to other Events, such as Message `header` MAY be used to provide the receiving application hints on how to handle the message payload.

Bindings

A "binding" (or "protocol binding") is a mechanism to define protocol-specific information. Therefore, a protocol binding MUST define protocol-specific information only.

Privacy and Security

Interoperability is the primary driver behind this specification, enabling such behavior requires some information to be made available **in the clear**, resulting in the potential for information leakage.

Consider the following to prevent inadvertent leakage especially when leveraging 3rd party platforms and communication networks:

- Standard Attributes

  Sensitive information SHOULD NOT be carried or represented in attributes.

  Events producers, consumers MAY introspect and log attributes.

- Data

  Domain specific Event Data SHOULD be encrypted to restrict visibility to trusted parties. The mechanism employed for such encryption is an agreement between producers and consumers and thus outside the scope of this specification.

- Protocol Bindings

  Protocol level security SHOULD be employed to ensure the trusted and secure exchange of Events.

Versioning

While updating a Event API to handle new or differing requirements is a relatively straightforward process, you must consider the effects that such changes will have on client applications consuming the Event API.
Your application need to follow the semantic version approach.

  | Type  | Description                                                  |
  | ----- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
  | MAJOR | when your event changes are incompatible.                    |
  | MINOR | when you add functionality in a backwards compatible manner.

|
  | PATCH | when you make backwards compatible bug fixes                 |

📒 See Event API versioning options

  | Guidelines                                                     | Severity | lint implementation status |
  | :------------------------------------------------------------- | :------- | :----------- |
  | 🚦 Always provide API version as major.minor.patch             |     MUST | Yes |
  | 🚦 Always include major version with event topic name          |   SHOULD |  |
  | 🚦 Always provide event version as major version only          |   SHOULD |  |
  | 🚦 Always provide Schema version as major.minor version only   |     MUST |  |
  
🎯 Make sure your consumer has clear understanding on your API versioning strategy

Deprecation

Sometimes it is necessary to phase out an API endpoint, an API version, or an API feature, e.g. if a field or parameter is no longer supported or a whole business functionality behind an endpoint is supposed to be shut down. As long as the API endpoints and features are still used by consumers these shutdowns are breaking changes and not allowed. To progress the following deprecation rules have to be applied to make sure that the necessary consumer changes and actions are well communicated and aligned using deprecation and sunset dates

  | Guidelines                                               | Severity | lint implementation status | 
  | :------------------------------------------------------- | :------- | :------------- |
  | Obtain Approval of Clients                               |     MUST |  |
  | External Partners Must Agree on Deprecation Timespan     |     MUST |  |
  | Reflect Deprecation in API Definition                    |     MUST |  |
  | Monitor Usage of Deprecated APIs                         |   SHOULD |  |
  | Onboarding new consumers to Deprecated APIs              | MUST NOT |  |
Core Design Principles

Overview

"Asynchronous," "push," or "streaming" APIs, which keep pushing data to the subscriber applications, are the foundation of "Event-enabled" APIs. This is a technique that enables consumers to subscribe to events or stream of events of interest. In other words, as an Event-API provider, it should allow its customers to subscribe to it, which should then asynchronously deliver events to them. Events are the foundation of EDA, and by using them, systems may react to events as they occur. With the usage of this method, the systems become independent, autonomous, flexible, agile, and scalable. In general an event is a change of the state of one entity, managed by the producer of the event. However, events can be further classified as Data Event, Business Event, and Command Event.

A system's data or information may change, causing a **Data Event**. The purpose of the (master) data events is to start a synchronization process with other systems that have a copy of the data.

Generally, the entire data entity with a UUID and version number is included in the data event. With the possible exception of analytical systems, master data event subscribers typically only care about the most recent version of the data entity. Usually the name of data events are determined by the data object that has changed and the Create/Update/Delete action that caused the event.

**Business Event** is triggered by process logic in the system. The event describes what happened. The event name will include a verb in the past sense (cancelled, deleted, shipped) that describes what happened, for example OrderDispatched, OrderCancelled. In a specific system, business events can be anything that has occurred. Business Events are used to inform other systems about a change that occurred in a system. With the **Command Event** it is the other way around. Instead of describing the other systems what happened, it instructs them on what to do. Command Events are used in the EDA and Microservices architecture to achieve eventual data consistency.

Business Event

- Business transaction triggered event
- Event describes which business transaction happened
- Verb in the past sense (cancelled, deleted, shipped) that describes what happened

Data Event

- Data entity change triggered event
- Master data changes that other software systems need to be aware of as they have a local or cached copy of data
- Data event can either contain the complete data entity or just the key and what sub-entity that changed. In the later case the subscriber, if needed, has to call a Web-API or Data-API to refresh it's own copy or cache.

Command Event

- Business transaction triggered event
- Used in Event Driven Architecture with the orchestration based SAGA pattern.
- The event doesn't tell the other system what happened, rather what action to perform.
- The saga orchestrator communicates with the participants using command/async reply-style interaction. The saga orchestrator sends a command message to a participant telling it what operation to perform.
- Action/Command (rejectOrder, deleteCustomer, dispatchOrder) indication. In that case is the field is typically a simple present tense verb.

---
Generic Design Principles

**MUST**: Follow Design First approach for an API developement

✅ **Rationale**

It is advantageous to define API designs iteratively before implementation for both people and machines that can understand APIs. In order to create an API contract that is acceptable to all stakeholders, each stakeholder should participate in the design of the API as the first step in this technique. This will ensure that each team uses the same tools and follows to the same API architecture for communication.

**RECOMMENDED**: Follow API First approach

✅ **Rationale**

The API first principle is an extension of design-first principle. Therefore, a development of an API MUST always start with API design without any upfront coding activities. API design (e.g., description, schema) is the master of truth, not the API implementation.

API implementation MUST always be compliant to particular API design which represents the contract between API stakeholder, implementers, and consumers.

**MUST**: Understand that APIs are (part of) the Product

✅ **Rationale**

It is expected to provide API to customers (internal and external) as products that can be used like a services in order to generate business value. Hence, the design of our APIs should be based on the API as a Product principle:

- Treat your API as product and act like a product owner
- Put yourself into the place of your customers; be an advocate for their needs
- Emphasize simplicity, comprehensibility, and usability of APIs to make them irresistible for client engineers
- Actively improve and maintain API consistency over the long term
- Make use of customer feedback and provide service level support

Embracing ‘API as a Product’ facilitates a service ecosystem which can be evolved more easily, and used to experiment quickly with new business ideas by recombining core capabilities. It makes the difference between agile, innovative product service business built on a platform of APIs and ordinary enterprise integration business where APIs are provided as “appendix” of existing products to support system integration and optimised for local server-side realization.

Understand the concrete use cases of your customers and carefully check the trade-offs of your API design variants with a product mindset. Avoid short-term implementation optimizations at the expense of unnecessary client side obligations, and have a high attention on API quality and client developer experience.

API as a Product is closely related to the API First principle.

**MUST**: Functionality and data exposed through standardized API contracts

✅ **Rationale**

An API contract is an agreement typically between a event producer and a subscriber that outlines the terms of use for an API. API agreements are essentially the documentation or specification for an API.

---
Compatibility
**MUST**: API contracts must be long-lived with a predictable lifecycle

**MUST**: Do not Break Backward Compatibility

✅ **Rationale**

APIs are contracts between producer and subscriber that cannot be broken via unilateral decisions. For this reason APIs may only be changed if backward compatibility is guaranteed. If this cannot be guaranteed, a new API major version must be provided and the old one has to be supported in parallel.

API designers should apply the following rules to evolve APIs for services in a backward-compatible way:

- Add only optional, never mandatory fields.
- Never change the semantic of fields (e.g. changing the semantic from customer-number to customer-id, as both are different unique customer keys)
- Input fields may have (complex) constraints being validated via server-side business logic. Never change the validation logic to be more restrictive and make sure that all constraints are clearly defined in description.
- Enum ranges can be reduced when used as input parameters, only if the server is ready to accept and handle old range values too. Enum range can be reduced when used as output parameters.
- Enum ranges cannot be extended when used for output parameters — clients may not be prepared to handle it.

However, enum ranges can be extended when used for input parameters.
- Use x-extensible-enum, if range is used for output parameters and likely to be extended with growing functionality. It defines an open list of explicit values and clients must be agnostic to new values.
On the other hand, API consumers must follow the tolerant reader rules (see below).

**MUST**: Clients must be Tolerant Readers

✅ **Rationale**

When building dependencies between teams and applications, we MUST focus on loose coupling. New versions of a dependency (API) MUST not be introduced, unless there is no other way. The evolution of an API MUST be compatible within one version as long as possible. Changes are breaking, when consumers need to change simultaneously. In that case a new version MUST be introduced and maintained. APIs should not have more than two concurrent supported versions.

---
Specification

**MUST**: Write New API Specification in AsyncAPI 2.X standard

✅ **Rationale**

We use the AsyncAPI specification as standard to define event-driven API specification files.
**MUST**: API must be written in British English
**MUST**: Consistency - Follow a common set of rules, interaction styles, vocabulary and shared types

**MUST**: Publish API Specification in Central Catalog

✅ **Rationale**

The API specification files should be subject to version control using a source code management system - best together with the implementing sources.

Producer MUST publish the API specification and make it discoverable. As a starting point, use INGKA Event API Catalog.

**MUST**: Use Semantic Versioning

✅ **Rationale**

Versions in the specification must follow the principles described by SemVer. Versions in queue/topic names are always major versions. Or in a more generic way: we avoid introducing minors or patches where it could break consumers compatibility. We explicitly avoid resource based versioning, because this is a complexity which an API should not reflect to its consumers. Refer more here for API versioning.

**SHOULD**: Publish API Schema in Schema Registry

✅ **Rationale**

If you are sharing data with clients outside the scope of your system, you will want to make sure that the schemas involved are actively managed. Doing so early in the pipeline will prevent your downstream clients from deserialization and processing issues at a later time (worstcase in production).

Schema Management involves publication, distribution and lifecycle control of message schemas published by asynchronous interfaces. Use a centralized schema management registry for

- creating, updating and deleting schemas
- sharing schemas, making them available to downstream clients
- implementing schema evolution rules to enforce compatible changes of the schema

**MUST**: Make API schema available for review

✅ **Rationale**

Publishing event API for use by others must make the event API schema definition, compatibility mode available for review.

---
Security

**MUST**: Secure API Endpoints

✅ **Rationale**

Every API endpoint (topic/queue) needs to be secured by an state of the art authentication mechanism supported by the platform you’d like to use.

- Oauth is the preferred option always for endpoints
- topic/queue MUST be supported by ACL

**MUST**: Do end to end security assessment of an API

---
Implemetation

**SHOULD**: Smart endpoints and dumb pipes

✅ **Rationale**

Each application owns its own domain logic. Outside of an application context, no transformation or business rules be applied. The infrastructure chosen is typically dumb (dumb as in acts as a message router only) – simple implementations to provide a reliable asynchronous fabric – the smarts still live in the endpoints that are producing and consuming messages. Functions that are necessary for filtering messages or for housekeeping tasks that can be managed directly in the context of an application.

**MUST**: Monitor API Usage

✅ **Rationale**

Owners of APIs used in production should monitor API usage to get information about its using clients. This information, for instance, is useful to identify partners for API changes and lifecycle management. As a starting point, use INGKA Event Catalog.

**MUST**: Support OpenTelemetry

✅ **Rationale**

Distributed Tracing over multiple applications, teams and even across large solutions is very important in root cause analysis and helps detect how latencies are stacked up and where incidents are located and thus can significantly shorten mean time to repair (MTTR).

To identify a specific request through the entire chain and beyond team boundaries every team (and API) MUST use OpenTelemetry as its way to trace calls and business transactions. Teams MUST use standard W3C Trace Context Headers, as they are the common standard for distributed tracing and are supported by most of the cloud platforms and monitoring tools. We explicitly use W3C standards for eventing too and do not differ between synchronous and asynchronous requests, as we want to be able to see traces across the boundaries of these two architectural patterns.

Traceparent
traceparent: ${version}-${trace-id}-${parent-id}-${trace-flags}

The traceparent header field identifies the incoming request in a tracing system. The trace-id defines the trace through the whole forest of synchronous and asynchronous requests.

The parent-id defines a specific span within a trace.

Tracestate
tracestate: key1=value1,key2=value2,…

The tracestate header field specifies application and/or APM Tool specific key/value pairs.

**MUST**: Support De-duplication

✅ **Rationale**

Event consumers must be developed to deal with duplicate messages. Most Message Brokers implement an at-least-once delivery strategy since exactly-once is usually too expensive.

When systems and networks behave correctly, messages are delivered only once. However, some circumstances might cause message duplication. For instance, a network glitch could avoid a message acknowledgment when a publisher sends a message. In that case, the publisher will resend the message, leading to a message duplication in the Message Broker. The same can happen on the consumer side, when the message cannot be acknowledged after a successful processing.

Consumers can follow one of these strategies to overcome this:

- Keeping track of the messages and discarding duplicates
- Write idempotent handling logic (although not always possible)

Refer here for message de-duplication in the Event Standards.

**MUST**: Test your API to ensure performance and functionality

✅ **Rationale**

**SHOULD**: Create events for every entity you maintain

Whenever a service provides read access to some entity for services from other contexts, it should also provide an event stream for this entity.

✅ **Rationale**

To reduce the number of synchronous GET calls, a service should subscribe to event streams to maintain a local representation of data owned by another service. The information provided by entities owned by a service should be completely recoverable from the appropriate event streams.

The same general design rules apply to both REST endpoints and events. The actions that modify an entity should result in appropriate events that describe the modifications.

A service maintaining a shopping basket allows read access. A basket consists of some general attributes and a list of line items. Additionally, a client can create a basket, add / remove line items and delete a basket. Thus, the service should provide an event stream for baskets. The events should contain all information that is accessible event API interface. After creation of a basket, a "basket-created" event should be published. After deletion, a "basket-deleted" should be published.
And, depending on the detailed design of modification interactions, the service should for example publish "basket-line-item-added" and "basket-line-item-removed" events upon modification.

**MUST**: Associate an event with *one and only one* root-entity

If your services maintain a hierarchy of entities where one type of entity is contained in another (object composition), events should be associated with the root entity.

This can also be guessed from the REST interface: If a resource has sub resources, the events should be associated with the root resource. Thus, even if only a property of a sub resource changes, an event for the whole aggregation should be published.

✅ **Rationale**

The transactional completeness of the root entity can't be guaranteed if it does not contain its sub resources.

To gain a good understanding of the theoretical background and the principles for designing entities that fit well into an event driven approach, look into the concepts of Domain Driven Design, esp. the concept of an Aggregate.

Looking at the basket example again, there should be only a basket topic and no basket line item topic. Even if just the quantity of a single line item changes, the event for the whole basket must be published.

**MUST**: Only represent changes that already happened

An event may only be published to the event broker (i.e. pub/sub, solace, kafka) once the transaction persisting the state change has successfully completed in the system.

✅ **Rationale**

Events are meant to distribute information about what changed in the persistent state of a service. Thus, they may be only published after the change.

**COULD**: Keep events focussed

The different changes of an entity should not be aggregated to one event. There should be one focused event for each change.

✅ **Rationale**

Consumers that want to react to a domain specific state transition can do this much better if they are not required to infer the transition based on the payloads attributes. Some consumers might even be able to not persist the payload or to skip certain events. This will lead to a more efficient overall system.

- ~~basket-updated~~
- basket-line-item-added
- basket-line-item-removed

**COULD**: Use domain specific events

Instead of using CRUD events like entity-created, entity-updated, entity-deleted you could use custom events that refer to the entity's domain.

✅ **Rationale**

Consumers that want to react to a domain specific state transition can do this much better if they are not required to infer the transition based on the payloads attributes. Some consumers might even be able to not persist the payload or to skip certain events. This will lead to a more efficient overall system.

As a producer, even when using domain specific event types, it is required to always send the full entity as payload, see Serialize events as JSON containing a header and the event payload.

As a consumer, beware the log-compaction (if applicabale) might lead to the situation where the remaining event is of an event type that is not of relevance and/or unknown. Consumers that maintain a local representation must consume all payloads independent of the event type, see Consume independent of the event type if a local representation is maintained

- ~~customer-created~~
- customer-registered

**MUST**: Keep events self-contained

All information necessary to "understand" an event must be contained in the event.

All information about the state of the entity after applying the change described by the event must be contained in the event.

The preferred way to achieve this is by including the whole representation of the entity in the event.

✅ **Rationale**

A consumer must have no need to make calls back to the event producing system while processing an event.

**MUST**: Keep events transactionally complete

Given a consistent state, after applying the event on an entity, the state of all published entities must still be consistent.

This limits both the design space for designing the entities as well as the space for designing the events.

✅ **Rationale**

Not following this rule will lead to the fact that a producer needs to publish more than one event to ensure a consistent state of all entities. But it is not guaranteed that consumers will process all related events in one transaction (as only the order, but not time of arrival of a message is guaranteed by message brokers). During that period of time a consuming service would hold an inconsistent state.

**COULD**: Exclude mutable or sensitive data from linked resources

Related resources that might be embedded in your REST response should not be included in your event payload.

It is a good practice to only publish the ID / candidate keys / address of a linked resource instead of all of its attributes. Immutable attributes like candidate keys (such as IDs in external systems, human readable names that increase analyzability) may be included as well. Mutable attributes should only be included if they are "frozen" for the rest of the entity's lifecycle.

✅ **Rationale**

Including mutable data in your topic forces you to send updates whenever an embedded entity changes. This can lead to a mass of updates, for example when many updates happen at once and many services embed data like this.

---
Event Format

**MUST**: Name events after the root entity and the change that happened

An event must be named with pattern \-\-\, e.g. basket-created-V1.

✅ **Rationale**

As every event is associated with a specific entity, the event should be named after this entity.

As events describe a change that has already happened, this change should also be included in the name.

As the event already should have happened, the change should be named in past tense.

Events associated with baskets should be named something like:

- basket-created-V1
- basket-deleted-V1
- basket-line-item-added-V1
- basket-line-item-removed-V1
- basket-line-item-quantity-changed-V1

**MUST**: Keep the format of all events within the same topic backwards compatible

If you need to make breaking changes in the event format, you must create a new topic for the new format.

Please make an effort to avoid breaking changes, as they result in unexpected work for all consumers.

In the exceptional case that a breaking change is absolutely required, you must create a new topic *version*. Events have to be published to both topics until no consumer is using the old version anymore. Consumers can be encouraged to migrate to the new topic version, but this may take longer than you might expect. The new topic name may include the event format version in the topic name, see Meet topic naming conventions.

✅ **Rationale**

When making changes to the event format, you can't expect consumers to update instantaneously. This rule helps consumers to migrate independently from releases of producers.

You should **not** e.g.

- remove mandatory attributes
- rename attributes
- change the semantics of existing attributes

---
Topic

**MUST**: Document your topic

Each topic must be documented in AsyncAPI and cataloged that other teams can easily decide whether and how to take advantage of consuming it.

Required information in the documentation are:

- topic name
- event types
- format of payload
- standard attributes/headers

**MUST**: Use an expressive topic name

Don't use cryptic abbreviations for your topic name, don't use technical terms for business topics. Refer more on topic naming here.

✅ **Rationale**

The topic name should allow potential consumers to decide whether they are interested in the events on this topic or not.

---
Producer

**MUST**: Publish events as soon as possible after related entities have been persisted

A producer must publish an event as soon as possible after it occurred.

✅ **Rationale**

We want to keep consumers as up to date as possible. The goal of the eventing system is that changes propagate in "near-real-time" through the services. Delaying the publication of an event increases the time until consistency is archived, as well as might lead to events queueing up thus creating the potential for load spikes in consuming services.

**MUST**: Care about the order in which events are received by the broker

A producer must ensure that events for the same entity are sent in order of occurrence.

A producer may implement a separate process which is taking care of publishing events in the correct order, e.g. using database locks.

✅ **Rationale**

The events are stored in order of being received by the broker. Due to network latency events sent to the topic may be processed in another order than expected.

**MUST**: Ensure that events aren't lost in case publishing the event fails

While it is very rare, it can happen that the event system (i.e. pub/sub, solace, kafka) is unavailable for an hour or more. Events that were supposed to be published during that time should not be dropped.

Having a simple in-memory retry would not be sufficient, because there is no guarantee that the service wouldn't be stopped while the event broker is still down. Instead you should persist the messages so that they can be resent once the event broker service is available again.

✅ **Rationale**

If events get lost, they have to be published manually which is inconvenient and might delay the message even further.

The only reason to not republish failed events is if the topic can cope with message loss without losing its purpose. For example this can be the case when the topic contains tracking or analytical data that is not about specific events but about the chronological sequence of events. But be aware: Sometimes the purpose is rather defined on the consumer side. So while the producer team thinks that a message loss is manageable the consumer team might oppose.

**COULD**: Store events in an event repository

The producer could store the raw event in a database to enable retries until it's committed to the cluster.

✅ **Rationale**

The producer has to make sure that the message is delivered and committed. Separate processing can take care of order, retries and cleanup.

**COULD**: Be able to re-publish the current state upon request

From time to time it is necessary that a publisher needs to do a "full dump" upon request.

✅ **Rationale**

This is a helpful feature in case of disaster recovery as well as after introduction of new attributes in the event format.

In case of disaster recovery **do not** increase the entity version without other entity changes. It might put a high load on downstream consumers, because it circumvents idempotence.

Please note, that it is not required to publish events for already deleted entities.

---
Consumer

**MUST**: Process events idempotently

Consuming the same event multiple times must not lead to data inconsistencies.

✅ **Rationale**

The eventing infrastructure (producers, brokers and consumers) guarantees "at-least-once" delivery. The same event might be consumed twice or more by a single consumer. Each consumer must be aware of this and the event processing must be idempotent.

**COULD**: Skip events due to older version number

Consumers may use the entity's version number to determine whether an event has already been processed and therefore can be skipped.

✅ **Rationale**

There are different situations why a message is sent more than once:

- Event broker ensures "at-least-once" delivery which means that message delivery is guaranteed but not necessarily once. Duplicate delivery can happen when e.g. a consumer goes down (failure, deployment, etc) before the acknowledging on receiving message.
- As broker does not forbid multiple deliveries, it can happen that a service/team delivers single messages (or in the worst case all messages) again.

There are some aspects you should consider when thinking about skipping or reapplying duplicate messages:

- First checking the recent version for an entity means most likely to implement an additional repository access. This can lead to slower consuming on high traffic topics
- Re-publishing on topic can be caused by faulty behavior in the publishing service. If this is a critical issue in your service you should consider skipping duplicates.

- Even though you need to implement consumers in an idempotent way handling duplicate messages can cause problems like distorted metrics or logs.
- When consuming events as triggers to start certain processes this can be crucial. If a second execution of the process is unwanted you should implement a skipping logic.

**COULD**: Skip events due to date

Consumers may use the date to determine whether an event is irrelevant for a specific process and therefore can be skipped.

✅ **Rationale**

When re-consuming a topic that contains ephemeral data e.g. after data loss, time to recovery is critical.

If by looking at a timestamp, a consumer can decide with certainty that a message does not have to be processed any more (e.g. picking for an order that would have been delivered yesterday), the message can be skipped.

This is preferable to version lookups, because the skip can be done very fast without performing even a single database roundtrip.

**COULD**: Write unprocessable events into a private error storage

If an event could not be processed, it might be a good idea to store this event in a private persistant store for later error handling. This private store for example can be implemented as an additional table in the database of the consumer. However the consumer MUST acknowledge the actual event delivery.

✅ **Rationale**

The error storage could be used for monitoring purposes, or for re-processing events after a bugfix in the consumer. Also helps if there exist poisen message.

Event Design Standards

---

- Event Design Standards
  - Event Data
  - Standard Attributes
    - Naming Conventions
    - Type System
    - REQUIRED Attributes
      - x-message-id
      - x-message-source
    - OPTIONAL Attributes
      - x-message-time
  - Example

Event Data

As defined by the term data, Events MAY include domain-specific information about the occurrence. When present, this information will be part of Message `payload`.

- Description:
    The event payload. This specification does not place any restriction on the type of this information. It is encoded into a media format which is specified by the `header` attribute (e.g. application/json), and adheres to the data schema format.

---

Standard Attributes

Every Events conforming to this specification MUST include attributes designated as REQUIRED, MAY include one or more OPTIONAL attributes. Each standard attribute MUST only appear at most once in an Event. The attributes defined within this specification are known as "core attributes". Attributes, while descriptive of the event, are designed such that they can be serialized independent of the event data. This allows for them to be inspected at the destination without having to deserialize the Event Data i.e `payload`. The attributes in Events specifications define mappings to various protocols and encodings, application headers and the accompanying Events SDK targets various runtimes and languages.

Naming Conventions

In order to maximize the likelihood of interoperability and portability across transport protocols and messaging formats, Events application defined custom attribute such as message `header` names MUST consist of lower-case letters ('a' to 'z') or digits ('0' to '9') from the ASCII character set. Attribute names SHOULD be descriptive and terse and SHOULD NOT exceed 20 characters in length.

Type System

The following abstract data types are available for use in attributes. Each of these types MAY be represented differently by different event formats and in protocol metadata fields. This specification defines a canonical string-encoding for each type that MUST be supported by all implementations.

- `Boolean` - a boolean value of "true" or "false".
  - String encoding: a case-sensitive value of `true` or `false`.
- `Integer` - A whole number in the range -2,147,483,648 to +2 147,483,647 inclusive. This is the range of a signed, 32-bit, twos-complement encoding. Event formats do not have to use this encoding, but they MUST only use `Integer` values in this range.
  - String encoding: Integer component of the JSON Number per
    RFC 7159, Section 6 optionally prefixed with a minus sign.
- `String` - Sequence of allowable Unicode characters.

The following characters are disallowed:
  - The "control characters" in the ranges U+0000-U+001F and U+007F-U+009F (both ranges inclusive), since most have no agreed-on meaning, and some, such as U+000A (newline), are not usable in contexts such as HTTP headers.
  - code points
    identified as noncharacters by Unicode.
  - code points identifying Surrogates, U+D800-U+DBFF and U+DC00-U+DFFF, both ranges inclusive, unless used properly in pairs. Thus (in JSON notation) "\uDEAD" is invalid because it is an unpaired surrogate, while "\uD800\uDEAD" would be legal.
- `Binary` - Sequence of bytes.
  - String encoding: Base64 encoding per
    RFC4648.
- `URI` - Absolute uniform resource identifier.
  - String encoding: `Absolute URI` as defined in RFC 3986 Section 4.3.
- `URI-reference` - Uniform resource identifier reference.

- String encoding: `URI-reference` as defined in
    RFC 3986 Section 4.1.
- `Timestamp` - Date and time expression using the Gregorian Calendar.
  - String encoding: RFC 3339.

All attribute values MUST be of one of the types listed above.
Attribute values MAY be presented as native types or canonical strings.

A strongly-typed programming model that represents a Events or any extension MUST be able to convert from and to the canonical string-encoding to the runtime/language native type that best corresponds to the abstract type.

For example, the `time` attribute might be represented by the language's native **datetime** type in a given implementation, but it MUST be settable providing an RFC3339 string, and it MUST be convertible to an RFC3339 string when mapped to a header of an HTTP message.

A Events protocol binding or event format implementation MUST likewise be able to convert from and to the canonical string-encoding to the corresponding data type in the encoding or in protocol metadata fields.

An attribute value of type `Timestamp` might indeed be routed as a string through multiple hops and only materialize as a native runtime/language type at the producer and ultimate consumer. The `Timestamp` might also be routed as a native protocol type and might be mapped to/from the respective language/runtime types at the producer and consumer ends, and never materialize as a string.

The choice of serialization mechanism will determine how the  attributes and the event data will be serialized. For example, in the case of a JSON serialization, the attributes and the event data might both appear within the same JSON object.

REQUIRED Attributes

The following attributes are REQUIRED to be present in all Events:

x-message-id

- Type: `String`
- Description:
  Identifies the event. Producers MUST ensure that `x-message-source` + `x-message-id` is unique for each distinct event. If a duplicate event is re-sent (e.g. due to a network error) it MAY have the same `x-message-id`. Consumers MAY assume that Events with identical `x-message-source` and `x-message-id` are duplicates. Refer Event API core design principle for getting the context of message de-duplication.
- Constraints:
  - REQUIRED
  - MUST be a non-empty string
  - MUST be unique within the scope of the producer
  - MAY included in Message `header`
- Examples:
  - An event counter maintained by the producer
  - A UUID

x-message-source

- Type: `URI-reference`
- Description:
  Identifies the context in which an event happened. Often this will include information such as the type of the event source, the organization publishing the event or the process that produced the event. The exact syntax and semantics behind the data encoded in the URI is defined by the event producer.

  Producers MUST ensure that `x-message-source` + `x-message-id` is unique for each distinct event.

  An application MAY assign a unique `x-message-source` to each distinct producer, which makes it easy to produce unique IDs since no other producer will have the same source. The application MAY use UUIDs, URNs, DNS authorities or an application-specific scheme to create unique `x-message-source` identifiers.

  A source MAY include more than one producer. In that case the producers MUST collaborate to ensure that `x-message-source` + `x-message-id` is unique for each distinct event.

- Constraints:
  - REQUIRED
  - MUST be a non-empty URI-reference
  - An absolute URI is RECOMMENDED
  - MAY included in Message `header`
- Examples
  - Universally-unique URN with a UUID:
    - `urn:uuid:6e8bc430-9c3a-11d9-9669-0800200c9a66`
  - Application-specific identifiers like INGKA SMC abbreviation, Store ID
    - `/FLI/STO-123`

OPTIONAL Attributes

The following attributes are OPTIONAL to appear in Events. See the Notational Conventions section for more information on the definition of OPTIONAL.

x-message-time

- Type: `Timestamp`
- Description:
  Timestamp of when the event happened. If the time of the event cannot be determined then this attribute MAY be set to some other time (such as the current time) by the Events producer, however all producers for the same `x-message-source` MUST be consistent in this respect. In other words, either they all use the actual time of the event or they all use the same algorithm to determine the value used.
- Constraints:
  - OPTIONAL
  - If present, MUST adhere to the format specified in
    RFC 3339

  ```yaml

  asyncapi: 2.4.0

    title: WMS Stock Adjustment Solace API
    version: 1.0.0
    description: Warehouse Management System - Stock Adjustment.

contact:
      email: owner@ingka.ikea.com
    license:
      name: Apache 2.0
      url: 'https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0'
  servers:
    Devlopment:
      url: 'tcps://server.messaging.solace.cloud:55443'
      protocol: solace
      description: Devlopment GCP Solace Broker
      security:
        - user-password: []
  defaultContentType: application/json
  channels:
    'ingka.ilo.ddo/stock/adjustment/sync/V1/GL/FLI/{BusinessUnitType}/{BusinessUnitCode}':
      description: WMS Stock Adjustments Event
      parameters:
        BusinessUnitCode:
          $ref: '#/components/parameters/BusinessUnitCode'
        BusinessUnitType:
          $ref: '#/components/parameters/BusinessUnitType'
      servers:
        - Devlopment
      subscribe:
        summary: Publish the Event related to the Event specification data.

operationId: wmsStockAdjustments
        bindings:
          solace:
            bindingVersion: 0.3.0
            destinations:
              - destinationType: topic
              - deliveryMode: persistent
            headers:
              type: object
              required:
                - x-DMQ-Eligible
              properties:
                x-DMQ-Eligible:
                  type: string
                  const: 'true'
                  description: >
                    > Solace standards flag for marking message as DMQ Eligible.
                    This header parameter not yet published as supported.

message:
          $ref: '#/components/messages/wms-stock-adjustment-v1'
      x-ingka-attributes:
        confidentiality: internal
        visibility: ikea-internal
        reusability: system
        status: draft
        event-mesh-visibility: global
  components:
    parameters:
      BusinessUnitCode:
        description: Business Unit Code
        schema:
          type: string
          minLength: 3
          maxLength: 5
          example: 8
      BusinessUnitType:
        description: Business Unit Type
        schema:
          type: string
          minLength: 2
          maxLength: 3
          example: CDC
    messages:
      wms-stock-adjustment-v1:
        name: wms-stock-adjustment-v1
        title: Stock adjustment schema for GIS
        description: Schema for the validation of stock adjustment messages.

contentType: application/json
        schemaFormat: application/vnd.aai.asyncapi+json;version=2.0.0
        headers:
          $ref: '#/components/schemas/customMessageHeaders'
        payload:
          $ref: '#/components/schemas/wmsStockAdjustments'
    schemas:
      wmsStockAdjustments:
        type: object
        title: Stock adjustment schema for GIS
        description: Schema for the validation of stock adjustment messages.

properties:
          LUStockStatus:
            type: object
            properties:
              StockStatus:
                type: string
              StockStatusSubCode:
                type: integer
                minimum: 0
                maximum: 99
            required:
              - StockStatus
            additionalProperties: false
        ConsignmentId:
          type: object
          properties:
            ConsignmentNumber:
              type: string
              maxLength: 12
              minLength: 1
          required:
            - ConsignmentNumber
          additionalProperties: false
      customMessageHeaders:
        type: object
        required:
          - x-message-id
          - x-message-source
        properties:
          x-message-id:
            type: string
            description: Unique message ID in scope of message producing System.
            example: bf45fd15-a599-428e-86b5-f8fc53112280
          x-message-source:
            type: string
            description: >
              > Identifies the context in which an event happened. Often this will
              include information such as the type of the event source.

example: /FLI/STO-123
          x-message-time:
            type: string
            description: Timestamp of the event occurance.
            example: 2019-10-12T07:20:50.520Z
    securitySchemes:
      user-password:
        type: userPassword
        description: Provide your username and password for authentication
  ```

Event-Mesh Standards

---

- Event-Mesh Standards
  - Core Principles
    - Producer
      - **MUST** Producer publishes on Topic for event distribution
      - **MUST** Producer ensures Dead Message Queue Eligibility of messages on Topic to TURE
      - **MUST** Producer ensures publishing mode PERSISTENT
      - **MUST** Producer respects NACK and resend messages
      - MUST own cross border topic subscription for spacial market
      - MUST declare private topic in client repo
    - Consumer
      - **MUST** Consume from a queue
      - **SHOULD** Use DMQ with a Queue to address discarded messages
    - Topic
      - Topic Naming Standards
        - **MUST** Define Topic Root
        - **MUST** Define Topic Properties

Properties
      - **SHOULD** Use wildcard expression for event filtering
    - Queue
      - **MUST** Include the System abbreviation as the queue name's prefix
      - **MUST** conform to the following characters in queue naming
      - **MUST** define Queue Size
      - **SHOULD** Use Non-Exclusive Queue
      - **MUST** Use Maximum Redelivery attempts
      - **MUST** Monitor Queue Size getting full
    - Implementation
      - **MUST** Tolerate connection disruption
      - **MUST** conform to Guaranteed Messaging
      - **MUST** Monitor connection capacity
      - **SHOULD** Use persistent connection with broker
      - Protocol
        - **SHOULD**  Use SMF
      - Messaging Pattern

- Messaging Pattern
        - **SHOULD** Use Publish-Subscribe

---
Core Principles
Producer

**MUST** Producer publishes on Topic for event distribution

✅ **Rationale**

Topics are a means of classifying information, and in practice they're simply strings that are composed of one or more levels. It's important to note that there are no topic resources to be created or configured on the messaging broker. To deliver enterprise-wide distribution of reusable events and event-driven architectures, the events must be addressable and routable to the applications that are interested. The event topic is used to address this. The event topic is simply additional information (or metadata) in the messaging layer header.

**MUST** Producer ensures Dead Message Queue Eligibility of messages on Topic to TURE

✅ **Rationale**

Even if the subscriber set up the DMQ correctly, the producer must make sure the DMQ Eligibility flag value is always set to `true` in order to prevent message discard. If and only if this flag is true on a message, the eligible messages can be forwarded from a queue to a DMQ.

Review that the Solace APIs have different default settings for this flag depending on the applicable versions.

📚 Read more : Reference

Example for publishing via SMF

```yaml

asyncapi: 2.4.0
info:
  title: WMS Stock Adjustment
  version: 1.0.0

...

channels:
  "ingka.ilo.ddo/stock/adjustment/sync/V1/ANY/FLI/{BusinessUnitType}/{BusinessUnitCode}":
    subscribe:
      summary: Publish the Event related to the Event specification data.

bindings:
      solace:
        bindingVersion: 0.3.0
        destinations:
          - destinationType: topic
          - deliveryMode: persistent
        headers:
            type: object
            properties:
              x-DMQ-Eligible:
                type: string
                const: 'true'
            
...

components:
  messages:
    wms-stock-adjustment-v1:

```

How to specify in Async-API Spec

Example for publishing via REST

```yaml

asyncapi: 2.4.0
info:
  title: WMS Stock Adjustment
  version: 1.0.0

...

channels:
  "ingka.ilo.ddo/stock/adjustment/sync/V1/ANY/FLI/{BusinessUnitType}/{BusinessUnitCode}":
    subscribe:
      summary: Publish the Event related to the Event specification data.

bindings:
      http:
          bindingVersion: '0.1.0'
          headers:
            type: object
            properties:
              Content-Type:
                type: string
                enum: ['application/json']
              Solace-DMQ-Eligible:
                type: string
                const: 'true'
              Solace-User-Property-x-message-id:
                type: string
            
...

components:
  messages:
    wms-stock-adjustment-v1:

```

**MUST** Producer ensures publishing mode PERSISTENT

✅ **Rationale**

Persistent messaging is also over TCP but has an additional set of acknowledgements beyond those used by TCP itself. When a message is sent as persistent, the broker acknowledges receipt of that message when the message is saved in an endpoint (like queue) on the broker. If the consuming application is not connected or cannot keep up with the publishing application, the messages are stored in endpoints. On the publishing side, the delivery mode is dependent on the message property `Delivery Mode`. This property can be set to `DIRECT` or to `PERSISTENT`. Publisher MUST use `PERSISTENT`.

Example how to set Delivery Mode in Solace Java API

> Message.setDeliveryMode(‘PERSISTENT’);

Published Guaranteed messages are acknowledged when they are received by the Solace PubSub+ event broker. Producer might receive acknowledgment for a single published Guaranteed message or a range of published Guaranteed messages by using the acknowledgment event mode (the acknowledgment for a given message implicitly acknowledges all preceding messages).

The broker must successfully receive messages regardless of subscription interest to those messages or not.

📚 Read more : Reference

```yaml
      status: draft
      event-mesh-visibility: global
components:
  messages:
    wms-stock-adjustment-v1:

```

📚 Read more : Reference

**MUST** Producer respects NACK and resend messages

✅ **Rationale**

There are several reasons a Solace event broker might reject and discard a message. For example, if the client username of the sending application does not have the proper ACL permissions to send the message to the defined topic. In such cases, the Solace broker sends a message rejection to the publisher, a.k.a. negative acknowledgment or “nack”, indicating that the message could not be persisted and was discarded. In situations like this, the publishing application may want to know about the error and act on it.

To avoid message loss, the producing application MUST detect NACK from the broker and handle the error and resubmitting the unpublished messages.

MUST own cross border topic subscription for spacial market

✅ **Rationale**

MUST declare private topic in client repo

✅ **Rationale**

---
Consumer

**MUST** Consume from a queue

✅ **Rationale**

  It is must to publish to a topic but consume from a queue, for using Guaranteed Messaging. Consuming from a queue means to bind to a queue.

  To consume all the messages fo interest, one has to add subscriptions to the queue. After doing that, the queue stores all messages with topics matching the subscriptions for consumption. The consumer will still be able to receive those messages if they go offline and then come back online.

  It is possible to add multiple subscriptions to one queue online or delete subscriptions from a queue online.

When to use it

  Use subscriptions to a queue every time you want to consume guaranteed messages.

**SHOULD** Use DMQ with a Queue to address discarded messages

✅ **Rationale**

  By default, Guaranteed messages are removed from a durable endpoint's (mostly, this is a **queue**) message persisted and discarded when:

- the number of redelivery attempts for a message exceeds the Max Redelivery value for the original destination queue
- or, a message’s TTL value has been exceeded, and the queue is configured to respect message TTL expiry times.
  
If you don't want the messages to be discarded, they can be moved to a dead message queue (DMQ) assigned to the queue.

When to use it

  Use Dead Message Queues, if you need to analyze every single message that is discarded.

How to configure / use it

- The publisher has to flag the messages as "DMQ Eligible"
- Create a Dead Message Queue
- Set the "DMQ"-field on the destination queue, to the name of the DMQ

📚 Read more : Reference

---

Topic

The flexibility and intelligence of the Event Mesh very much depends on the information available in the hierarchically structured topic definition. The event topic is part of the **message header** and the flexibility and power of the event mesh dynamic routing and filtering relies on the information in the event topic.

A **Topic hierarchy** is a hierarchical classification of information done in a similar way as in a web URL or directory structure. Keeping with these concepts, we want to produce hierarchical topics that rank from **least** specific to **most** specific. To decide whether to add a field into a topic, consider whether it will provide topic functionality. Can it be used for example routing, filtering, access control, or handling policies? If it's not likely to be used for any of these purposes, it doesn't need to be in the topic. Next, try to determine which fields are more specific than others by thinking about variability. The most variable fields are usually the most specific.

This naturally leads to a topic hierarchy that has the most variable items (web pages or files in the examples above) at the end of the topic string. This in turn makes it much easier to use wildcarding as a method of grouping like topics.

Topic Naming Standards

This describes how to name Event Topics in context of the enterprise level Event Mesh Reference Architecture.

**MUST** Define Topic Root

✅ **Rationale**

Event topic root of an event must have the following form

> DataEntityNamespace/DataEntityName/DataScope/Verb/Version

Refer topic root as mentioned in Topic Naming Standards

**MUST** Define Topic Properties

✅ **Rationale**

Event topic MUST include have the following properties

- Event Region
  - Constraint - Use `ANY` as default value if not applicable. No impact from platform point of view for using any other value.
- Event Originator

Refer topic properties as mentioned in Topic Naming Standards. Other topic properties MAY included in topic and are dynamically filled when the producer publishes the event.

Objective of including topic properties

- Enables ACL where publisher can publish event
- Prevents impersonation in event published on the topic root
- Subscribers are still able to know who published an event while listening on topic root with wild card subscription
- More granularity in topic provides more capbility in event filtering  

Topic definition

> DataEntityNamespace/DataEntityName/DataScope/Verb/Version/EventRegion/EventOriginator/{ObjectID}/{RelatedObjectID}/{AnyField}/...

Example

```text

- ingka.isom.sys/consignment/returnOrder/created/V1/ANY/ISOM
- ingka.erix.sys/RetailItemServiceEffort/full/updated/V1/ANY/ERIX
- ingka.cps.sys/fulfillmentOrder/statusUpdate/picked/V1/ANY/CPS
- ingka.fin.ado/customerTransaction/full/created/V1/CN/CTM

```

**SHOULD** Use wildcard expression for event filtering

✅ **Rationale**

  The main key for sending and receiving messages is the topic, that is a string attached to every single message that describes the content of the message and can be used for filtering the relevant messages for application.

  For publishing, topic is not allowed to include any wildcards.

  For receiving messages, one can use wildcards in as subscriptions.

  The following table is a short summary of wildcard characters in SMF and MQTT subscriptions (beware: MQTT uses a different syntax for wildcards).

|  SMF wildcard | MQTT wildcard |   Explanation                                     |
| :------------ | :------------ | :------------------------------------------------ |
|       *       |       *       |  Everything on that level                         |
|       xxx*    |       xxx*    |  Everything on that level, starting with xxx      |
|       >       |       #       |  Everything on that level and every level below   |

Example

```text

- ingka.dsm.ado/consignment/full/created/V1/*/DSM/*/*
- ingka.dsm.ado/consignment/full/created/V1/*/DSM/CTE-*/>
- ingka.cps.sys/fulfillmentOrder/statusUpdate/picked/V1/>

```

---
Queue

**MUST** Include the System abbreviation as the queue name's prefix

✅ **Rationale**

- Discoverability of queues for a System
- Assists with the creation of tools for validating queue ownership and applying system-specific rules.

  |    Field                |    Sevierity     | Description                                                       |
  | :---------------------- | :--------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------- |
  | Event Consuming System  |   MUST           | The system abbreviation name as defined in System Master Catalog. |
  | AnyField                | OPTIONAL         | To be defined by the consumer as per their business needs         |

**MUST** conform to the following characters in queue naming

Contain only the following characters: Letters [A-Za-z], numbers [0-9], dashes `-` , underscores `_` , periods `.` , forward slash `/`. Use only the above-mentioned list of characters that the messaging broker or platform supports.

**MUST** define Queue Size

✅ **Rationale**

We can configure how much disk space messages in a queue can consume at maximum.

If the "max-spool-usage" is reached, all new messages will be discarded until the consumer(s) have cleared / deleted messages from the queue. Default queue size is 5 GB.

Calculate the initial queue quota/size like this

```text
max spool size for queue =  *  *  * 1.2

```

burst message rate: What is the maximum rate at which publishers can publish messages that are subscribed by this queue ?

maximum downtime for consuming application: Consider re-deployment and application errors in the night or till max 3 days for use cases like analytical solutions, which typically react on the following working / business day.

You should increase this value, if

- you haven't 7x24 operations for your application
- you can't afford to loose any message
- the burst message rate is unknown / unsure
- the distribution of message size is wide spread... worst case, all burst messages during application downtime are large messages
- your consuming application processes messages slower than the burst publishing rate - in this case, you need the queue as store for messages even in normal operations.

**SHOULD** Use Non-Exclusive Queue

✅ **Rationale**

  Multiple consumers can bind to a non-exclusive queue. Each consumer is serviced in round-robin fashion. This provides load-balancing; however, if a connection fails, then unacknowledged messages are delivered to another consumer with the re-delivered flag set. In this way, messages can be delivered to consumers out of order. A non-exclusive queue allows you to add / remove application instances for messaging processing online without disruption.

When to use

  Use a non-exclusive queue, if you need multiple application instances to process all your messages in time. A non-exclusive queue is a kind of load balancing method for messages.

When **not** to use it?

  Don’t use a non-exclusive queue, if you need to process your messages strictly in order.

How to configure / use it
Just set the `exclusive`-flag for a queue to `false`.

**MUST** Use Maximum Redelivery attempts

✅ **Rationale**

  If not set, the broker tries to redeliver a message infinitely.

  You can set the queue parameter "max-redelivery" to a value between 0-255. If this is set and the broker has already tried that many attempts to deliver that message to a consumer, the broker will move that message to the DMQ (if defined and available) or discard it.

  Use that parameter to handle the risk of poisoned messages that could break your application.

When to use it

  Recommend setting of this parameter is to a sensible value like 5 or 10.

How to configure / use it

- Set the max-redelivery parameter for your consumer queue to a value.

**MUST** Monitor Queue Size getting full

✅ **Rationale**

To prevent message loss, application team needs to handle "queue quota is getting full" events before the queue is really full and messages will be discarded.

In normal operations, the queue usage should always be near zero.

To get enough time for handling errors (like analyzing, restarting application, fixing application errors or even re-sizing the queue for a larger quota), configure a low monitoring threshold to send alert.

Recommendation: Threshold at 20% for consumer queues. It should ideally be reviewed and set to a value that matches the criticality and preferences of the application team.

How to configure / use it

  Configure thresholds in application monitoring.

---
Implementation

**MUST** Tolerate connection disruption

✅ **Rationale**

  Must configure application to reconnect automatically to the broker in case of connection failure.

- Recommend settings:
  - connect retries: 10
  - reconnect retries: 20
  - reconnect retry wait: 3,000 ms
  - connect retries per host: 5

Note, the default values for connection retries, and retry wait times etc. differ among the Solace APIs

📚 Read more : Configuring Connection Time-Outs and Retries

**MUST** conform to Guaranteed Messaging

✅ **Rationale**

  Guaranteed Messaging, also known as Assured Delivery, provides at-least-once message delivery.

  Guaranteed messages are maintained in a non-volatile store;  therefore, Guaranteed messages are most appropriate for messaging applications that require persistent storage of the messages they send or intend to receive.

- Guaranteed messages have the following characteristics:

  - they cannot be unintentionally lost once they are acknowledged by the event broker
  - they cannot be reordered in the event of network topology changes
  - when they are delivered to a durable endpoint, they are retained until they are consumed by a client
  - they are acknowledged upon delivery to a client
  - The quality of service offered by Guaranteed Messaging is analogous to JMS persistent messaging with durable subscriptions.

📚 Read more : Supported message delivery mode
**MUST** Monitor connection capacity
**SHOULD** Use persistent connection with broker
Protocol

**SHOULD**  Use SMF

✅ **Rationale**

  The Solace Message Format (SMF) is Solace’s own protocol to send and receive messages with a Solace broker.

  Solace provides enterprise messaging Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) that enable you to develop applications for use with Solace PubSub+. Each API includes sample applications, release notes and developer documentation.

- APIs are provided for
  - Java, C#/.NET, JavaScript, Node.js, iOS, C and Java RTO:
  - C API—This API provides high message throughput and low latency with the lowest possible CPU utilization.
  - C#/.NET API—This API is an object-oriented, managed wrapper for the C API.
  - Go API—This API enables cloud-based and enterprise-scale server-based applications to use Solace messaging.
  - iOS API—This API is an iOS native wrapper of the C API specifically designed for high message throughput and low latency. It is also fully integrated with iOS application life cycle.

- Java API—A 100% pure Java implementation, the Java API is an object‑oriented API that delivers high message throughput.
  - Java Real-Time Optimized (RTO) API —This API is a low-latency Java Native Interface (JNI) wrapper for the C API.
  - JavaScript Messaging API—This API enables Web and mobile applications to Solace messaging.
  - Nodejs API—This API enables server side Web-connected enterprise applications to use Solace messaging and take advantage of the event-based programming enabled by Node.js.
  - Python API—This API enables cloud-based and enterprise-scale server-based applications to use Solace messaging.

These APIs  support all common message exchange patterns.

- Publish / Subscribe
- Point-to-Point
- Request / Reply

These APIs support all common API features.

- Connection management to Solace PubSub+ event brokers
Addition and removal of topic subscriptions
- Sending and receiving messages Direct and Guaranteed messages
Structured data types that do not rely on a specific architecture or programming language
- Extensive support for logging and customizing through property parameters
- Request / Reply messaging support Session Transactions
- PubSub+ Cache Client API support
- Queue browsing
- Message Replay

When to use it

  Use SMF wherever possible. SMF is the only feature complete API to Solace brokers. If you have no specific reason to use any other protocol (e.g. because some integration framework does only support something other than SMF), use SMF.

How to configure / use it

- On the broker side, no additional configuration is needed to get SMF to work.
- For the application side, download and use the API for your language.

- API Overview
