Event API Confidentiality

 Confidentiality relates to restricting access to information and solutions to authorized persons only, based on work role, need to know, clearance etc. A breach of confidentiality could result in information being disclosed to unauthorized parties.

API Confidentiality Enumerations with details description

  | Type  | Description |Enumerations|
  | :--- | :--- | :---|
  | Public                | All information is generally accessible to everyone.|**public** |
  | Internal              | Information which may or must be accessible to all co-workers. |**internal**|
  | Confidential          | Information which is only accessible to a limited group of users.|**confidential**|
  | Strictly Confidential | This concerns sensitive data which should only be made available to the immediate recipient. |**strictly-confidential**|
  
---

Detail explanation
  
- Event API Confidentiality
  - API Confidentiality Enumerations with details description
  - Detail explanation
    - public
    - internal
    - confidential
    - strictly-confidential

---

public

- All information is generally accessible to everyone.
- Breach of this classification is not possible
- No protections required against exposure and access
- Label/tag document/e-mail with an appropriate disclaimer and classification level

internal

- Information which may or must be accessible to all co-workers.
- Confidentiality is low.
- Violation of this classification can cause some direct or indirect damage.
- Single-sign-on (SSO)
- Segregation of duties implemented using role-based access control
- Separation of environments between production and non-production environments

confidential

- Information which is only accessible to a limited group of users. Information is made available based on trust.
- Breach of this classification can cause serious direct or indirect damage.
- Controls are in place to limit exposure to a predefined group of individuals only (e.g. members of a specific project or business unit), as defined by the Information Owner
- Access limited to authorized recipients
- Encryption for data transferred outside IKEA
- Approval from the document owner is required before sharing

strictly-confidential

- This concerns sensitive data which should only be made available to the immediate recipient.
- Breach of this classification can cause (very) extensive damage.
- Controls are implemented to limit exposure to one or a very few individuals only, as defined by the Information Owner
- Access can only be granted to co-workers and must be approved from the relevant management level
Event/API lifecycle flow

API lifecycle

  | State      | Description|Enumerations|
  | :--- | :--- |:---|
  | Draft   | Services which is under development with a high possibility to become a provided service |**draft**|
  | Published | Services that are available today for consumer |**published**|
  | Deprecated | No new subscriber should be allowed to subscribe to that API. Requests from existing consumers will be fulfilled as usual. There could be a chance that the another version of same events may exist and over the time all the existing consumer should be migrated and started consuming new version of the events |**deprecated**|
  | Retired | Services which is going to be stopped after an agreed date. It means before that date all consumer need to move to the alternate service. | **retired**|

---

Below picture will show, how lifecycle will move from one state to another in DPDR model

!lifecycle

| current state  | Next possible state|
| :--- | :--- |
| Draft   | Published or Retired |
| Published | Deprecated or Retired |
| Deprecated | Published or Retired |
| Retired | can't move any state |

API Reusability

 API classified from the reusability perspective of how well an API is aligned with INGKA level policies, scope (boundary), standards and guidelines related to design of canonical, system independent APIs.
!reusability

API Reusability Enumerations with details description

  | Type                  | Description                             |Enumerations            |
  | :-------------------- | :-------------------------------------- |:---------------------- |
  | Business API          | A Business API is a reusable Published API that has a distinct capability within a corporate domain that can be functional or technical                                           |**business**            |
  | System Specific API   | A System Specific API is a Published API that is based on System specific data models and design guidelines shared by a single or limited group of consumer systems.          |**system**              |
  | Single Purpose API    | A type of API that is designed for use by a specific API consumer such as a frontend application or a partner/B2B system                                                        |**single-purpose**      |

---

Detail explanation

- Business API
- System Specific API
- Single Purpose API

---

business

- A Business API is a reusable Published API that has a distinct capability within a corporate domain that can be functional or technical
- A Business API has a lifecycle that is independent of any software system that implements the API
- A Business API is designed using system independent API schema - standards and technologies such as Open API/Swagger
- A Business API is designed to be part of an Ingka API Catalog that should be perceived as an engineered whole by API consumers
- A Business API follows strict design policies and guidelines related to naming, security, logging, header etc.

- A Business API can be based on any type of API Style (see previous section)
- A Business API must be based on a corporate Data Concept model
- A Business API can be based on a detailed, canonical Data Entity model that represents core API schema payload objects
- Business API design responsibility can sit with team that owns a core system that implements the Business API or with a separate "Canonical API design team" on domain level
- Business API design and evolution is governed by domain level architects/engineers
- Business APIs represent a key concept for managing system landscape modernization in terms of keeping APIs and substituting underlying software systems

system

- A System Specific API is a Published API that is based on System specific data models and design guidelines shared by a single or limited group of consumer systems
- A System Specific API does not have  not expected to live longer than the specific software system
- From an Ingka perspective vendors and partners typically provide System Specific API. In some cases these external APIs can be "promoted" into Business APIscan be  A System

single-purpose

- A type of API that is designed for use by a specific API consumer such as a frontend application or a partner/B2B system
- Single Purpose APIs cannot be reused

Visibility flow

API visibility is all about target audience of your API in terms of discoverability and subscription availability. If you API is discoverable by certain user base but until they can subscribe and call your API, the categorization would be justified.

API visibility detail

| Category           | Description                                                                                                                   | Enumerations       |
| :----------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :----------------- |
| Private API        | An API implemented by an Software Component that is only exposed to other Software Components within the same Software System | **private**        |
| Ingka internal API | A Published API that is only made available to Software Systems that are owned by Ingka organizations                         | **ikea-internal**  |
| Ikea partner API   | A Published API that is only made available to Software Systems owned by Partner to IKEA                                      | **ikea-partner**   |
| Ingka internal     | A Published API that is made available for ingka internal                                                                     | **ingka-internal** |

---

Here is the pictorial view of API visibilities.

!visibility
Detail explanation

Private API

- An API implemented by an Software Component that is only exposed to other Software Components within the same Software System.
- Depending on system level architecture and development framework, Private APIs are typically further classified into different concepts/levels such as “protected” or “friend” scope.

Published API

- An API implemented by an Software Component and that is exposed towards Software Components in other Software Systems.
- Implemented and published using any type of networked API technology (typically internet based)
- Note that a Published API can be designed independently of the actual software components that implements the API. See API Reusability Classification below.

IKEA Internal API

- A Published API that is only made available to Software Systems that are owned by Inter and Ingka organisations.

IKEA Partner API

- A Published API that is only made available to Software Systems owned by Partner to IKEA
- An IKEA Partner API can be used by a single Partner or a group of Partners (also see API Reusability classification below)
- Partner can be any type of B2B integration and can also represent an external company that is partially or fully owned by Inter or Ingka

INGKA Internal API

- A Published API that is only made available to Software Systems owned by INGKA Internal

Enumerations used for visibility

- private
- ikea-internal
- ikea-partner
- ingka-internal
Reusability

Api Reusability Classifications - Business & System

We have introduced the possibility to tag your API accordingly to the corresponding API Reusability Classifications while publishing or updating your api. Simply add "reusability": "value" to the request body. There is two potential classification values to choose from, system and business. If none is passed while publishing the value will default to 'business'.

Business API

- A Business API is a reusable Published API that has a distinct capability within a corporate domain that can be functional or technical
- A Business API has a lifecycle that is independent of any software system that implements the API
- A Business API is designed using system independent API schema standards and technologies such as Open API/Swagger
- A Business API is designed to be part of an Ingka API Catalog that should be perceived as an engineered whole by API consumers
- A Business API follows strict design policies and guidelines related to naming, security, logging, header etc.

- A Business API can be based on any type of API Style (see previous section)
- A Business API must be based on a corporate Data Concept model
- A Business API can be based on a detailed, canonical Data Entity model that represents core API schema payload objects
- Business API design responsibility can sit with team that owns a core system that implements the Business API or with a separate "Canonical API design team" on domain level 
- Business API design and evolution is governed by domain level architects/engineers
- Business APIs represent a key concept for managing system landscape modernization in terms of keeping APIs and substituting underlying software systems

System Specific API

- A System Specific API is a Published API that is based on System specific data models and design guidelines shared by a single or limited group of consumer systems
- A System Specific API does not have  not expected to live longer than the specific software system
- From an Ingka perspective vendors and partners typically provide System Specific API. In some cases these external APIs can be "promoted" into Business APIscan be  A System

Data Concept

We have introduced the ability to assign the corresponding Data Concept that belongs to your API while publishing or updating. Simply add "dataConceptName": "value" to the request body.

Definition

Data concepts are the information artefacts that describe the data used in processes.  They are used for finding and understanding data at a conceptual level within process context and means for communication. It sets the boundaries/ scope of data to be detailed out at logical and physical level after which it can be implemented into systems.

Data concepts distinguish different levels of abstraction and require a taxonomy to capture relations amongst them. They are not pointing to logical data entities or attributes. Data concepts are unique and facilitate re-use of data across the IT landscape. All data concepts must be defined with terms and described in the business glossary. Terms within the business glossary can reflect many artefacts at different levels, thus are not always data concepts.

Why do we need Data Concept and what is the value

- Data Concepts help establish common vocabulary to enable effective communicate in system agnostic language across the different parts (technical/Non-Technical) parts of the Organisation
- Data Concepts can also serve as Input and Output to the Business process modelling at various levels of processes.
- Data Concepts helps in the software design process and System Implementation.
- Data Concepts is key building block for Data Democratisation through Data Marketplace.
- A collection of Data Concept can also be used to monitor and govern the KPIs on the Data quality, Data classification or Data governance of company data asset.
- Data Concepts contribute to Interoperability, i.e. the "I" in F.A.I.R. By using the same name and definition for a piece of data across domains, we will understand each other. By designing business API:s  according to naming and meaning of Data Concepts, our systems can communicate in an interoperable way.
- Data Concepts help in the communication of responsibilities for data. One digital domain is responsible for certain Data Concepts, another domain is responsible for other Data Concepts.

It must be clear who is responsible for what data.

Read more - https://confluence.build.ingka.ikea.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=310833620

If you don't know you Data Concept

Ask your manager och Tech Lead / Architect, or you can search in the Data Catalog - https://datacatalog.ingka.com/

API Lifecycle

We have now made it available for users to send in the lifecycle along with the publishing of the API. Simply send it in your request body as "lifeCycleState": "value". If not passed, the value will default to "published". The values that is available to choose from is, draft, published, deprecated & retired.

Draft
Services which is under development with a high possibility to become a provided service

Published
Services that are available today for consumer

Deprecated

No new subscriber should be allowed to subscribe to that API. Requests from existing consumers will be fulfilled as usual. There could be a chance that the another version of same events may exist and over the time all the existing consumer should be migrated and started consuming new version of the events

Retired

Services which is going to be stopped after an agreed date. It means before that date all consumer need to move to the alternate service.
Business WebAPI Compliance Indicator- REST

Motivation

API Management and Event management teams are working with different engineering domains to bring acceptable API standards for both 'Web API' and 'Async API'. The outcome of such engagement is API standards and best practices which are written and maintained in Ignka API Standards.

The engaged community also worked on a linting tool that encapsulated all the rules, which are possible of validate and then validate one API specification in different stages of API development and deployment lifecycle. The linting tool would be available to use as a github workflow and as a CLI tool.

The last piece of the puzzle to bring API uniformity and standardization is to place a solution that leverages the agreed API standards in a seamless manner during the API deployment process to central API catalog and gives a better visibility of below measurement in Allen portal

- API specification standard compliance
- API implementation compliance
- API performance & health compliance

For the current MVP realease we considered only 'API specification standard compliance'.

How the compliance indicator works

The business web API compliance indicator works based on two set of user provider data during API publication to API catalog (with or without API deployment to APIM managed gateway)

- API Specification
- API metadata

API Specification:

The API is the most important factor in API compliance calculation. The Rules are mentioned in Ignka REST WebAPI Standards. You can check the value of a column called 'lint implementation status' to understand if the rules are already part of current implementation or not.The implentation of API specification liniting is based on redocly CLI and enhancement is done based on rules mentioned in Ignka REST WebAPI Standards. To follow all the rules redockly based linter enforced follow [Lint OpenAPI]https://github.com/ingka-group-digital/lint-openapi.

The lint-openapi returns API validation results with different severity (warn, error etc). The compliance indication has defined weightage for them as shown below.

| severity | weightage |
| :------- | :-------- |
| error    | 3         |
| warning  | 1         |

while calculating the overall score total violation is calculated based on count and weightage and then deducted from 100. If the total score is less than 100 then it would remain as 0 as -ve score is not shown.

Example 1: If there are 3 errors and 10 warnings in and api doc then:

                Final Score = 100 - ( 3* number of errors + 1* number of warnings)
                Final Score = 100 - ( 3*3+ 1*10)
                Final Score = 100-19
                Final Score = 81
                
 Example 2: if there are  40 error and 20 warning in an api doc then:
 
                Final Score = 100 - ( 3* number of errors + 1* number of warnings)
                Final Score = 100 - ( 3*40+ 1*20)
                Final Score = 100-140
                Final Score = 0 (since your score is coming in -ve) .

API Metadata

API metadata is Ingka business structure related properties which are softly tagged with API while publishing to API Catalog. Right now there are three set of API metadata which are part of API compliance indicator

| property                                              | weightage |
| :---------------------------------------------------- | :-------- |
| system from system master catalog                     | 3         |
| team name from myidentity (aka IGNA)                  | 3         |
| Data concept from Ingka business data concept catalog | 1         |

While publishing through the APIM catalog API system and team name are part of the onboarding process, hence data concept is the only input driven field among three
while publishing through APIM Provider API all three fields are user input driven fields. Data concept calculations is not applied to system APIs.

Fixing compliance issues

Depending on the scope of the issue either you need to update your specification or you need to add API business metadata

Fixing API specification

Please go through all the violated points and understand them. You can follow Ignka REST WebAPI Standards to know more. If you are considering to modifiy the rules then please raise a pull request or discussion in the github repo itselfIf you plannning to adhere exsiting rules then once your specification is changed then you can validate them using Lint OpenAPI. It support three ways of API spcification validation

- github actions
- CLI
- npm package

🎯 If you are using Kong API gateway managed by APIM then there are some additional rules which can be found here

(want to contribute to release another form of validation please raise one discussion in the GIT repo or reach out to APIM team)

Once you fix it please republish the specification.

Fixing API metadata

Simply use APIM catalog API or APIM provider API request body to update API metadata

We have introduced a new endpoint which can be used to validate your specs before publishing it. This will give you chance to work on your errors and warning and get rid of them before it is published and shown to the consumer of your api.

Publish REST API in APIM Catalog

APIM team offers APIM Catalog API that API provider/owner use to deploy API to managed API Catalog.

Deployment Steps

Get raw url of your API specification file and other markdown files.

Open the file in github.com and click on raw tab present in the right side and get the url till the string '?token'.

Get the Authorization header:

Use the 'ClientId' you received while onboarding.

Prepare the request body for API document publish:

You need to carefully form the requestbody for API deployment. Read the APIM Catalog API for more details of each fields of request body

```
curl -X 'POST' \

  -H 'accept: */*' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'X-Client-Id: ' \
  -d '{
  "specUrl": "",
  "environment": "", // dev,stage or prod
  "onboardingDocUrl": "",
  "apiAccessDocUrl": " ",
  "dataConceptName": "
}'

```

How to use it?

As a GitHub action

Add the following step to your GitHub workflow job:

> **_Recommendation:_** run it before publishing the API to Kong API Gateway

```yaml
- uses: ingka-group-digital/lint-asyncapi@v1.1.0
    with:
      files: |
        
        
```

As a GitHub workflow

You can create a separate GitHub workflow to lint-asyncapi spec files:

> **_Recommendation:_** run it whenever a new change in the spec files is pushed

```yaml
name: Lint AsyncAPI files

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
  push:
    paths:
      - 

jobs:
  lint-asyncapi:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - name: Lint AsyncAPI specifications
        uses: ingka-group-digital/lint-asyncapi@v1.1.0
        with:
          files: |
            
            
```

As a CLI tool

You can run the linter locally by executing the package with `npx` and passing the path to a yaml AsyncAPI specification file.

> **_Recommendation:_** use as a CLI and pass the path to the AsyncAPI specification .yaml file

`npx @ingka-group-digital/lint-asyncapi ./examples/asyncapi-01.yaml`

As an npm package

You can install the linter as npm package and run it in your own application, npm package is deployed in Ingka JFrog Artifactory. To install the npm package in your application need to follow below steps:

1. You need username and password to install and publish a module in Jfrog registry. you can request a username and auth token via Allen

2. Need to have an \_auth token which can be achived by running below command

```
curl -u {{ username }}:{{ password }} https://artifactory.build.ingka.ikea.com/artifactory/api/npm/auth

```

3. create a .npmrc file in the root of your application as below, replace the ${TOKEN} with \_auth token generated in above step.

```
always-auth = true
@ingka-group-digital:registry=https://artifactory.build.ingka.ikea.com/artifactory/api/npm/ingka-npm-shared-local/
//artifactory.build.ingka.ikea.

ikea.com/artifactory/api/npm/ingka-npm-shared-local/:_auth=${TOKEN}
registry=https://registry.npmjs.com

```

3. to install the package run below command

```
npm install @ingka-group-digital/lint-asyncapi
```

4. import the module in your file and lint the asyncapi spec as below

```
import { asyncapiLint } from '@ingka-group-digital/lint-asyncapi';
const stringifiedAsyncApiSpec = `asyncapi: 2.6.0
info:
  title: Account Service
  version: 1.0.0
  description: This service is in charge of processing user signups :rocket:
channels:
  user/signedup:
    subscribe:
      message:
        $ref: '#/components/messages/UserSignedUp'
components:
  messages:
    UserSignedUp:
      payload:
        type: object
        properties:
          displayName:
            type: string
            description: Name of the user
          email:
            type: string
            format: email
`;

What is it?

`lint-asyncapi` is a GitHub action, CLI tool and npm package that helps you find problems with your AsyncAPI specification and ensure that it follows INGKA API standards maintained by API Management team.

How it works?

This GitHub action runs a script on the provided AsyncAPI spec file. The script analyzes the file, checks it against list of rules and shows possible problems. Under the hood it uses `@stoplight/spectral-core` npm package for linting the file and configuring these rules.

> **_NOTE:_** Every rule has a `severity` property which defines the severity level of the problem if the assertion is false. Possible values are: `error`, `warn` and `info`.
>
> In case of a negative assertion:
>
> - If a rule uses `error` severity, the problem will be printed out and then the pipeline will fail.
> - If the rule uses `warn` severity, the problem will be printed out but the pipeline will continue.
> - If the rule uses `info` severity, the suggestion will be printed out but the pipeline will continue.

Which rules are used in this action?

Current set of rules contains recommended rules from the `spectral` package and the custom rules implemented on top of the recommended ones. Both of them together form the rules which conform INGKA API standards provided by the EVENTM team. All rules are listed in asyncapiRulset under the `rules` property.

Recommended rules (34)

What is it?

`lint-openapi` is a GitHub action, CLI tool and NPM package that helps you find problems with your OpenAPI specification and ensure that it follows INGKA API standards maintained by API Management team.
How to use it?

As a GitHub action

Add the following step to your GitHub workflow job:

> **_Recommendation:_** run it before publishing the API to Kong API Gateway

```yaml
- uses: ingka-group-digital/lint-openapi@v1.0.1
    with:
      files: |
        
        
      shouldApplyGatewayRules: true
      shouldFailOnWarning: true
```

As a GitHub workflow

You can create a separate GitHub workflow to lint-openapi spec files:

> **_Recommendation:_** run it whenever a new change in the spec files is pushed

```yaml
name: Lint OpenAPI files

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
  push:
    paths:
      - 

jobs:
  lint-openapi:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - name: Lint OpenAPI specifications
        uses: ingka-group-digital/lint-openapi@v1.0.1
        with:
          files: |
            
            
          shouldApplyGatewayRules: true
          shouldFailOnWarning: true
```

Installing

You will need to add an npm registry entry to your `.npmrc` and set up Artifactory access in order to `npm install` the packages from this repository.

Artifactory access

In order to download these libraries, you will need to configure Artifactory access on your local machine. In order to do so, you will need to create the `ARTIFACTORY_AUTH_TOKEN` environment variable containing your Artifactory auth token.

Add the following to `$HOME/.bashrc` or `$HOME/.zshrc` file:

```shell
export ARTIFACTORY_AUTH_TOKEN=___PASTE_YOUR_AUTH_TOKEN_HERE___
```

How do I get my Artifactory auth token?
  

Generate your personal API key on the Artifactory profile page.

With the API key, get your personal credentials by running the following:

```shell
export ARTIFACTORY_USERNAME=___PASTE_YOUR_USERNAME_HERE___
export ARTIFACTORY_API_KEY=___PASTE_YOUR_API_KEY_HERE___

curl -u $ARTIFACTORY_USERNAME:$ARTIFACTORY_API_KEY https://artifactory.build.ingka.ikea.com/artifactory/api/npm/auth
```

And copy `_auth` value from the returned response.

Alternatively, you may create the auth token via:

```shell
export ARTIFACTORY_AUTH_TOKEN=$(echo -n $ARTIFACTORY_USERNAME:$ARTIFACTORY_API_KEY | base64)
```

Please note, however, `base64` may not be available on your environment.

How it works?

This GitHub action runs a script on the provided OpenAPI spec file. The script analyzes the file, checks it against list of rules and shows possible problems. Under the hood it uses `@redocly/openapi-core` npm package for linting the file and configuring these rules.

> **_NOTE:_** Every rule has a `severity` property which defines the severity level of the problem if the assertion is false. Possible values are: `error`, `warn` and `off`.
>
> In case of a negative assertion:
>
> - If a rule uses `error` severity, the problem will be printed out and then the pipeline will fail.
> - If the rule uses `warn` severity, the problem will be printed out but the pipeline will continue.
> - If the rule uses `off` severity, this rule would be ignored.

Which rules are used in this action?

Current set of rules contains recommended rules from the `@redocly/openapi-core` package and the custom rules implemented on top of the recommended ones. Both of them together form the rules which conform INGKA API standards provided by the APIM team. All rules are listed in redocly.yaml under the `rules` property.

Recommended rules (26)

| Rule                                 | Severity | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| ------------------------------------ | -------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `info-license`                       | warn     | Requires the license info in your API definitions. Read more                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| `info-license-url`                   | warn     | Requires the license URL in your API definitions. Read more                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| `tag-description`                    | warn     | Requires that the tags all have a non-empty `description`. Read more                                                                                                                                                                           |
| `no-path-trailing-slash`             | error    | Ensures that paths in your API do not end with a trailing slash (`/`). Read more                                                                                                                                                        |
| `no-ambiguous-paths`                 | warn     | Ensures there are no ambiguous paths in your API definitions. Read more                                                                                                                                                                     |
| `path-declaration-must-exist`        | error    | Requires definition of all path template variables. Read more                                                                                                                                                                      |
| `path-not-include-query`             | error    | Path should not include query parameters.

The query parameters should be defined on the `PathItem` or `Operation`. Read more                                                                                                            |
| `path-parameters-defined`            | error    | Requires all path template variables are defined as path parameters. Read more                                                                                                                                                         |
| `operation-2xx-response`             | warn     | Ensures that every operation in your API document has at least one successful (200-299) HTTP response defined. Read more                                                                                                                |
| `operation-4xx-response`             | warn     | Ensures that every operation in your API document has at least one error (400-499) HTTP response defined. Read more                                                                                                                     |
| `operation-operationId`              | warn     | Requires each operation to have an `operationId` defined. Read more                                                                                                                                                                      |
| `operation-summary`                  | error    | Enforce that every operation has a summary. Read more                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| `operation-operationId-unique`       | error    | Requires unique `operationId` values for each operation.

Read more                                                                                                                                                                |
| `operation-operationId-url-safe`     | error    | Requires the `operationId` value to be URL safe. Read more                                                                                                                                                                      |
| `operation-parameters-unique`        | error    | Verifies parameters are unique for any given operation. Read more                                                                                                                                                                  |
| `security-defined`                   | warn     | Verifies every operation or global security is defined. Read more                                                                                                                                                                             |
| `no-unresolved-refs`                 | error    | Ensures that all `$ref` instances in your API definitions are resolved. Read more                                                                                                                                                           |
| `no-enum-type-mismatch`              | error    | Requires that the contents of every `enum` value in your API definition conform to the corresponding schema's specified `type`. Read more                                                                                                |
| `spec`                               | error    | Ensures that your API document conforms to the OpenAPI specification. Read more                                                                                                                              |
| `no-invalid-media-type-examples`     | warn     | Disallow invalid media type examples by ensuring they comply with the corresponding schema definitions.

Read more                                                                                                               |
| `no-server-example.com`              | warn     | Prevents using `example.com` as the value of the `servers.url` fields in your API definitions. The rule checks for all URL schemes (`http`, `https`...). Read more                                                                       |
| `no-server-trailing-slash`           | error    | Disallow servers with a trailing slash. Read more                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| `no-empty-servers`                   | error    | Requires the `servers` list is defined in your API. Read more                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| `no-example-value-and-externalValue` | error    | Ensures that `examples` object properties `externalValue` and `value` are mutually exclusive. Read more                                                                                                                     |
| `no-unused-components`               | warn     | Ensures that every component specified in your API definition is used at least once. In this context, "used" means that a component defined in the `components` object is referenced elsewhere in the API document with `$ref`. Read more |
| `no-undefined-server-variable`       | error    | Disallow undefined server variables.
Read more                                                                                                                                                                                    |

Custom rules (9)

| Rule                                       | Severity | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| ------------------------------------------ | -------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `openapi-version-3xx`                      | error    | Requires the `openapi` property in your API definition. Version must be 3xx.                                                                                                                                                |
| `no-verbs-in-path`                         | warn     | Ensures that paths contain only nouns, no verbs. For example, it should be `/orders` instead of `/create-orders`.                                                                                                           |
| `info-description`                         | error    | Requires `info` object to contain a `description` property.                                                                                                                                                                 |
| `info-contact`                             | error    | Requires the `Contact` info object defined in your API. Read more                                                                                                       |
| `info-contact-email`                       | error    | Requires the `Contact` info email defined in your API.                                                                                                                                                                      |
| `operation-4xx-problem-details-rfc7807`    | warn     | Ensures that every operation with (400-499) HTTP response has content-type `application/problem+json` and fields `title` and `type`.

Read more |
| `request-media-type-map-application-json`  | warn     | Ensures only `application/json` is used in requests.                                                                                                                                                                        |
| `response-media-type-map-application-json` | warn     | Ensures only `application/json` is used in 2xx responses.                                                                                                                                                                   |
| `sever-description-contains-keywords`      | error    | Requires server descriptions to contain an environment keyword. Valid values are: `TEST`, `STAGE` and `PROD`.                                                                                                               |

Custom rules of Gateway (Kong) (4)

| Rule                             | Severity | Description                                                                                                                |
| -------------------------------- | -------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `base-path-has-valid-format`     | error    | API Specification has a base path and it should not have any special character, only alphabet, numbers and '/' are allowed |
| `base-path-has-valid-name`       | error    | API base path can't be empty or can't start with '/api' or '/virts'                                                        |
| `resource-path-has-valid-format` | error    | Resource path should not have any special character, only alphabet, numbers and `/`, `{`, `}`, `.`, `-`, `_` are allowed   |
| `title-has-valid-format`         | error    | Title should not have any special character, only alphabet, numbers and `-`, `_` are allowed                               |

How to use Custom rules of Gateway

For cli tool you need to pass the second parameter as true after the given filepath. Ex:

```sh
npm run start -- ./examples/openapi-01.yaml true
```

For npm package you need to use the function lintFromString and send the second parameter (shouldApplyGatewayRules) as true. Ex:

```sh
lintFromString(stringifiedSpec, true)
```

If you want to use the rules in github action you need to pass the second parameter as true after the filepaths in your workflow:

      files: |
        examples/openapi-01.yaml
      shouldApplyGatewayRules: true

How to add and configure existing rules?

You can use any rule which comes out of the box of `redocly` package. Full list of those rules may be found here. In order to override the severity of the rule which is being added via the `recommended` config or include a `redocly` rule which is not in the `recommended` config, simply add it to the redocly.yaml under the `rules` property like this:

```yaml

redocly.yaml

rules:
  # Overriding severity of the rule from the recommended config.
  security-defined: warn

  # Adding a `redocly` rule which was not in the recommended config.
  info-contact: error
```

It is also possible to create custom rules.

redocly.yaml

rules:
  # Custom rule starts with an `assert/` prefix. Prefix is followed by a unique rule ID which you come up with yourself.
  assert/info-description:
    # Subject object specifies which property of which data object needs to be asserted.
    subject:
      type: Info
      property: description

    # Message is the problem message that will be shown in case the assertion is false.
    message: Info object must contain a `description` property which has minimum of 30 chars length.

    # List of assertions. You can have combine multiple assertions
    assertions:
      # Asserts if a property is defined. Comes out of the box.
      defined: true

      # Asserts a minimum length of a string.
      minLength: 30

      # Custom assertion starts with a `custom/` prefix. Prefix is followed by a function name from `custom-rules-plugin.js`.
      custom/checkIfContainsKeywords:
        # In the custom assertion options you may pass as many parameters as you want.
        # In this example we pass one `keywords` option which is an array of strings.
Development

Install dependencies

```sh
npm i
```

Develop

You can start working with the codebase. In order to validate whatever you have developed run the linter locally:

```sh
npm run start -- ./examples/openapi-01.yaml
```

Create a PR

When you are ready to create a PR, don't forget to **build** and **package** the linter before that:

```sh
npm run all
```

Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct

Our Pledge

We as members, contributors, and leaders pledge to make participation in our
community a harassment-free experience for everyone, regardless of age, body
size, visible or invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender
identity and expression, level of experience, education, socio-economic status,
nationality, personal appearance, race, religion, or sexual identity
and orientation.

We pledge to act and interact in ways that contribute to an open, welcoming,
diverse, inclusive, and healthy community.

Our Standards

Examples of behavior that contributes to a positive environment for our
community include:

* Demonstrating empathy and kindness toward other people
* Being respectful of differing opinions, viewpoints, and experiences
* Giving and gracefully accepting constructive feedback
* Accepting responsibility and apologizing to those affected by our mistakes,
  and learning from the experience
* Focusing on what is best not just for us as individuals, but for the
  overall community

Examples of unacceptable behavior include:

* The use of sexualized language or imagery, and sexual attention or
  advances of any kind
* Trolling, insulting or derogatory comments, and personal or political attacks
* Public or private harassment
* Publishing others' private information, such as a physical or email
  address, without their explicit permission
* Other conduct which could reasonably be considered inappropriate in a
  professional setting

Enforcement Responsibilities

Community leaders are responsible for clarifying and enforcing our standards of
acceptable behavior and will take appropriate and fair corrective action in
response to any behavior that they deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive,
or harmful.

Community leaders have the right and responsibility to remove, edit, or reject
comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, and other contributions that are
not aligned to this Code of Conduct, and will communicate reasons for moderation
decisions when appropriate.

Scope

This Code of Conduct applies within all community spaces, and also applies when
an individual is officially representing the community in public spaces.
Examples of representing our community include using an official e-mail address,
posting via an official social media account, or acting as an appointed
representative at an online or offline event.

Enforcement

Instances of abusive, harassing, or otherwise unacceptable behavior may be
reported to the community leaders responsible for enforcement at
.
All complaints will be reviewed and investigated promptly and fairly.

All community leaders are obligated to respect the privacy and security of the
reporter of any incident.

Enforcement Guidelines

Community leaders will follow these Community Impact Guidelines in determining
the consequences for any action they deem in violation of this Code of Conduct:

1. Correction

**Community Impact**: Use of inappropriate language or other behavior deemed
unprofessional or unwelcome in the community.

**Consequence**: A private, written warning from community leaders, providing
clarity around the nature of the violation and an explanation of why the
behavior was inappropriate. A public apology may be requested.

2. Warning

**Community Impact**: A violation through a single incident or series
of actions.

**Consequence**: A warning with consequences for continued behavior. No
interaction with the people involved, including unsolicited interaction with
those enforcing the Code of Conduct, for a specified period of time. This
includes avoiding interactions in community spaces as well as external channels
like social media. Violating these terms may lead to a temporary or
permanent ban.

3. Temporary Ban

**Community Impact**: A serious violation of community standards, including
sustained inappropriate behavior.

**Consequence**: A temporary ban from any sort of interaction or public
communication with the community for a specified period of time. No public or
private interaction with the people involved, including unsolicited interaction
with those enforcing the Code of Conduct, is allowed during this period.
Violating these terms may lead to a permanent ban.

4. Permanent Ban

**Community Impact**: Demonstrating a pattern of violation of community
standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior,  harassment of an
individual, or aggression toward or disparagement of classes of individuals.

**Consequence**: A permanent ban from any sort of public interaction within
the community.

Attribution

This Code of Conduct is adapted from the [Contributor Covenant][homepage],
version 2.0, available at
.

Community Impact Guidelines were inspired by [Mozilla's code of conduct
enforcement ladder](https://github.com/mozilla/diversity).

[homepage]: https://www.contributor-covenant.org

For answers to common questions about this code of conduct, see the FAQ at
. Translations are available at
.

Introduction

This repository contains documentation, guides and principles related to the Ingka Retail Platform.

Read more about the goals and context here.

Ambition

The Ingka Retail Platform aims at providing a platform that can make several generations of systems work well together, and by combining them we will be able to support both traditional company needs, as well as new digital needs.

This documentation aims to guide teams to how to design, build, operate and maintain systems for the Ingka Retail Platform (IRP).

Contributions

This documentation is maintained by the Enterprise Architecture team at Ingka.

However, we are strongly embracing and encouraging Inner Sourcing practices so that we can grow and evolve the guidance together.

 See CODEOWNERS and CONTRIBUTING for more information.

 Also, to contribute, you need to set up a local environment to enable checks & validations. See the DEVELOPMENT guide on how to set this up.

About this documentation

This documentation will help guide and explain how the platform concept works and what is expected from teams accountable for delivering the systems in our landscape. See it as our **Platform Developer Kit** for IRP.

The Platform Developer Kit contains the following sections:

|Section|Description|

We believe that a principle driven approach to software, with guidance, is the best way to accomplish the outcomes needed.

Application of this documentation

Ingka Retail Platform is the common direction for our system landscape in Ingka, and covers Data, Tech, DX and Product dimensions. It is applicable to all systems within the Ingka Retail system landscape.

Guidelines for existing systems

Existing systems needs to apply this guidance when changes are made to the system. We don't expect all systems to be rewritten in a one-off effort to align with this direction.

Requirement levels

We align to the following RFC related to requirement levels: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2119

Contributions

We gladly accept contributions from anyone eager to make this guidance better. Please look at CONTRIBUTING.md for contributions and DEVELOPMENT.md for content development guidance.

Background

In 2018 Ingka assessed its current state of affair - both in terms of  how to meet our new strategies but also how to accelerate the digital deliveries as we where behind competitors and how to address key digital capabilities that was missing in order to become truly customer centric. We had up till then built up over decades tailored business processes with fit home-grown systems targeted for a Cash & Carry business with web and a mobile app on the side. In the last 10 years we delivered towards an IT strategy, focused on building a Tech Landscape based on buying software from the big dragons and, to the largest extent, outsourced application development as the method for lowering cost, while gaining capacity to manage an increased business development portfolio. Essentially it was a "Manage & Control" organisation we had built up - not a "Develop & Operate".

At this point in time, the term **'modernisation'** got traction, as Ingka looked at how to address the missing digital native capabilities and a Tech Landscape that could not deliver on the expected speed and flexibility to be relevant to the customer.

Given the high attention to address the technical debt and a required enabler for the company, the Digital strategy included the modernization need as well, which got crafted during this period. That contributed to be a catalyst and focus.

The sense of urgency and getting a rapid and radical change built the business case and became part of INGKAs larger company change - which came to be known for the many as Lead, Work and Organise (LWO).

F1

During end of 2018 and spring of 2019, an initiative was formed, called F1, aimed at exploring and defining an approach including patterns for how to tackle the technical debt and what needed to be set in place as a foundation. Three different approaches was developed and then external vendors was approached and included. This to get external perspectives but also to address how to scale the work, since Ingka didn't have any engineering capability more than literally a handful of engineers - and it would take time to build it up and have our own thought leadership.

One part of validating the vendors, was to give then real tasks to perform actual development to tackle modernization challenges. Their choice of pattern, design and code quality got evaluated. These were our first steps to establish new products and a catalyst to get us going and deliver value. In parallel LWO designed the operating model and organisation for the new Group Digital.

As part of the assessment, **we classified our systems if we should migrate and decommission systems** and it also became the starting point to make its possible to describe a clear direction for each system and prioritisation of work. It was also concluded that just replacing old technology and systems with new ones would not necessarily make us more successful in a digital world - but rather understand first the new demands and requirements. That led us to our customers and have that as a starting point to the decide what to decommission.

**Digital is about a great customer experience, and it was clear that our vision for the customer experience should be the driving force, and setting the priority for the work we referred to as modernisation**. At the same time, it was also concluded that working on the technical debt needed to start in parallel to establishing and building up a new Digital organisation, who would set and deliver to the actual vision for the customer experience.

F1 identified and started to address technical enablers that wouldn't be sunk cost as they needed to be addressed regardless, given that Ingka would be bringing development back in-house and building an engineering organization.

The modernisation came to be about changes to software as a primary focus and not hardware or infrastructure. Looking back at our challenges, we saw that most of these was related to how the systems was designed, not how they where run, where and on what infrastructure.

Through the F1 activity we where able to pilot technical enablers, find technical patterns for how to continue our work with becoming more digital. Likewise how to move from systems to services. These patterns and enablers has since then been reused and enhanced to become our Technology Foundation, that is part of our Digital Strategy.

From Systems to Services

We have had a system-centric design of our landscape historically, which in some way has become introvert. This means that we have developed systems with a lot of focus on the inner workings and design, and with less consumer needs in focus. By shifting our rhetoric to talk about services, we have started thinking more about how data is consumed, in addition to how it's managed inside a system. This leads us to become more customer focused all the way through our value chain.

It also drives us to understand that the interfaces and interactions between systems are very important. We need to standardise these interfaces to be able to move faster, and become more resilient throughout the landscape. This is why we are using terms such as API-First, Event Driven Architectures and more. It brings focus to the interactions during our designs.

Thus, going from a system-centric thinking to service-centric, means we lay a foundation for speed, flexibility and increased stability throughout our landscape.
How did we get here and where are we?

Past & Future

What IKEA does as a business is defined through Business Capabilities. I.e. what we need to be able to do as a company to be successful. If we think of all the business capabilities as being either:

* Traditional, i.e. solving age old IT company problems
* Digitised, i.e. same process, new technology
* Digital, i.e. new processes, new value propositions

By putting our Business Capabilities, Traditional, Digitised or Digital, as the vertical axis and time on the horizontal axis we can describe our current and future progress as below.

How we have evolved

What we have seen, and what we have in part intentionally guided our teams towards can be described by further separating the Digital part into several parts. We have driven the idea of creating new services, that works in a new way, pushing the idea of Business APIs, and have been able to create new experiences based on that. But some of our new digital products have not followed this separation. This is starting to become problematic and an impediment for scaling our business.

Foundations for a new architecture

Our first Digital Strategy 1.0 has given us pointers to what the new architecture needs to be. With external input and validation on proven models for retail we see that we have started to shape a new architecture.

Based on how have evolved we can see that some patterns and layers are becoming clearer. These patterns and layers are proven in Digital Retail Businesses. We need to clarify our architecture, so we do this purposefully and consciously. This evolution has become our new architecture, the **Digital Foundation Architecture** - named as **Retail Business Platform**.

Business API

What is the purpose of a Business API?

The overall purpose and value of a Business API is to contribute in delivering "a flexible and adaptable landscape of data and technology" and "build end-to-end digital foundation for IKEA" as stated by Digital Strategy 2.0.

In practice a Business API:

* exposes a core Ingka software capability that is reusable across the IKEA digital ecosystem
* is designed based on enterprise level capabilities and data representations with a longer lifetime than a specific implementation
* provides the surface of the Ingka Retail Platform that hides implementation and modernization complexity

From a "vision" perspective the following conceptual view illustrates how Business APIs are structured into architecture domains for reuse across the Omnichannel customer journey software.

!Business APIs in context of Omnichannel Platform
Key messages in diagram:

* An Ingka Retail Platform providing reusable Business APIs to any type of analytical and operational consumers in Omnichannel context
* Business APIs are designed in context of the Ingka Architecture Area model

What are the characteristics of a Business API?

A Business API can be defined in the following way:

> A Business API exposes a distinct Ingka software capability that is reusable across the IKEA digital ecosystem and is designed to have a longer lifetime that an individual implementation.

The following diagram provides some more details on the key characteristic of Business APIs as designed independent of implementing software.

!Business API are System independent

Summary of Business API characteristics:

* A Business API is designed to be independent and have a longer lifetime than the individual software and system that implements the Business API specification
* A Business API can be implemented using different API Styles depending on consumer needs.
  * Business Web API based on industry patterns such as REST or GraphQL and using standard Ingka API Gateway and API Catalog products.

* Business Event API that publishes of Event topics in line with Ingka Event Mesh standards
  * Business Data API that provides general, direct database API in line with Ingka Data Mesh and Data Product standards
* A Business API represents a distinct enterprise level capability within the Ingka digital ecosystem
  * Enterprise level software capabilities are modelled in the Architecture Area model. Also see FAQ - Architecture Areas.
* Business API name and schema elements is aligned with corporate Data Concept and Data Entity models.
  * Established Data Concepts can be found in Data Concept Glossary and Ingka Data Model.
* A Business API follows enterprise level design guidelines and standards related to naming, versioning and security.
* A Business API is published and discoverable using an enterprise level API Catalogue.
* A Business API is implemented based on Ingka level technology standards in order to achieve low costs, scalability, monitoring,  performance ,and reduces modernization/refactoring efforts.
* A Business API has a design and implementation that has actively considered NFR needs and CAP theorem
* A Business API is not a "backend for frontend" API.
Read more on the rationale and implication of these characteristics in *What are the characteristics of a Business API?* in FAQ - What is a Business API?

How do you design a Business API?

The initial identification and design of Business APIs is done in the early stages of a software engineering process that addresses a specific business need ("API First").
Later in the development process the Business API specification is detailed and implemented by one or more components and systems.

So what differs the design process of a Business APIs from any other category of APIs? The differences can be summarized as:

* The Business API specification is aligned with enterprise level capability and data models to ensure distinct and consistent APIs and data representation
* The Business API implementation and publication is aligned with certain enterprise level technology standards in order to secure NFRs and make it easy for consumers to consume APIs

The following diagram provides a simplified overview of the activities involved in designing a Business API and the relationship to the enterprise level Architecture Area software capability model.

!Business API are System independent

Key messages in diagram:

* **Understand Business Need** includes analysis of specific requirements as well as the context of the IKEA Business Capability Model
* **Determine needed Software Capabilities** in Ingka context is done in the context of the enterprise level Architecture Area model
* **Determine needed Business API Capabilities** designs Logical Business APIs based on the Architecture Area capabilities and Data Concepts in the Ingka Data Model
* **Design Business API Specification** including the naming of actual Business API specification and schema elements are based on the Architecture Area Logical Business APIs and any existing standard/canonical Data Entity models
* **Implement Business APIs** based on software layering patterns and technology standards connected to Ingka Retail Platform

For more details on the Business API design process and activities, see:

* Checklist - Business API Design
* FAQ - What does Business API mean in Software Engineering context?
Choreography & Orchestration

Purpose

Often when a business process is executed, multiple systems are involved that need to create or update data as part of that process.
Also it can be the case that a data concept that is changed are part of the process in one system might need to refer to data in another system.
This type of coordination is in the software domain often referred to as a Saga.

Two basic types of coordination can be applied to design such Saga's:

* Choreography
* Orchestration

While they might seem alike at first glance they are actually quite different in practice.

Choreography

**Compare this model with a dance performance.**

Agreements are made up front on the total choreography.
Each dancer (system) performs it own steps, and reacts to others (e.g. keeping enough distance).
When deviations from the plan occur, each dancer needs to decide upon adjustments themselves to keep the show going.

This favours autonomy of the actors, local decision making and emergent overall system behaviour.

Choreography is inherently asynchronous and reactive in nature

!Choreography

Orchestration

**Compare this model with an orchestra performance.**

Performers play to the notes and the partiture defined by the composer. For the execution there is a conductor, that continuously monitors and adjust what performer do by keeping a close eye on what is happening, and giving signals to adjust the performance through his baton. Deviations from the plan will be managed by the conductor, and the performers follow in suit.

This favours centralised decision making and central design on the desired overall system behaviour.

Orchestration can be set up synchronously as well as  asynchronously. The stable factor is that Commands will we sent from a central system that acts as the conductor for the process.

!Orchestration

Comparison

In practice, you will often need a combination of both ways. This blog has a nice write-up of pro's and con's of the approaches.

For reference a short summary here as well in the table below.

| Aspect | Orchestration | Choreography |
|--|-------------|--------------|
| Design-time complexity | Low | Medium |
| Design-time autonomy | Low | High |
| Easy of change | Low | High |
| Coordination responsibility | Central | Decentral |
| Run-time autonomy | Low | High |
| Process visibility | High |  Low |
| Troubleshooting complexity | Medium | High |

See the diagrams below to highlight the differences between Choreography, Synchronous Orchestration and Asynchronous Orchestration.

---

!Choreography



*Systems integrated using Choreography*

---

!Sync Orchestration

*Systems integrated using synchronous orchestration*

---

!Async Orchestration

*Systems integrated using asynchronous orchestration*
