Change Management for APIs

The purpose of this guide is to provide guidelines for API change management.

Why change management for APIs

While designing and developing APIs the following aspects in the context of change management are important to consider for ensuring the success of an API:

1. An API is not stable; it will change. Our business is changing so do the APIs providing the data and capabilities to support our business. Our APIs need be able to follow the rate of change in the business.
2. To follow the business, implemented API changes should be made available to consumers when needed. A platform API can have multiple consumers using the API in different contexts. We need to be able to apply changes to our APIs with minimal friction and dependency management between the API producer and its consumers.

To enable the above mentioned needs we need to define a change management strategy for APIs.

References

As inspiration for creating our API versioning guidelines we used the following reference as inspiration:

* Semantic Versioning
* Nordics APIs - API Change Strategy

Furthemore, where relevant references are included to specific sections in our Ingka API standards:

* Ingka API Deprecation standard
* Ingka API Lifecycle rules standard
* Ingka Web API versioning standard
* Ingka Event API versioning standard
API Change Management Principles

MUST: Strive for backwards compatibility

A good practice is to introduce changes by not breaking the API interface but introduce the change as an extension; non-breaking change. In this way you provide **backwards compatibility** towards existing consumers and new features become available in the API interface without breaking consumers.

Some examples of breaking changes:

* You add a new mandatory field in an API input message
* You remove a field in an API input or output message
* You change existing business logic of the API implementation causing the API to behave differently; a semantic change.

we must strive for **backwards compatibility** by adopting the following extension rules:

* You **must not** take anything away
* You **must not** make optional things required
* Anything you add **must** be optional

We need to be realistic and realize that we cannot always stay backwards compatible. Trying to stay backwards compatible can have a bulldozer effect on complexity. Sometimes you need to make the decision for the better to make a clear cut and apply a breaking change to avoid unnecessary complexity.

In order to not break the consumer immediately the old and new interface version will run in parallel to give time to consumers to migrate to the new version. But we also don't want to run old versions forever to avoid unnecessary ops complexity and costs so we need to define an end-of-life moment for older versions.

MUST: Apply a deprecation policy for breaking changes

A good practice is to establish a clear and reasonable deprecation policy for older version of the API to keep the landscape clean. Clearly communicate when a version will be deprecated and when it will be completely removed so consumers understand when they need to have migrated to a new version. Provide guidance on migrating to newer MAJOR versions and give consumers a reasonable amount of time to do so.

See also the API Standards on API Deprecation

SHOULD: Use a communication plan to announce API changes

Clearly communicate versioning changes to developers. Use release notes published through known communication channels to inform the developer community about upcoming changes, deprecated features, and the introduction of new versions.

MUST: Apply the semantic versioning scheme to the API product

Versioning is a means to manage API changes, do dependency management and communicate on changes towards consumers.

Versioning needs to be applied with care to make it manageable and not end up in a nightmare.

Semantic Versioning scheme is a clear and intuitive version scheme that we must adopt.

Below it is prescribed how to adopt the Semantic Versioning scheme:

|Version|Increments|
|-------|-----------|
|MAJOR | Increment to introduce a breaking change; resets the MINOR and PATCH version to 0 |
|MINOR | Increment to introduce new functionality in a backward compatible manner |
|PATCH | Increment to introduce backwards compatible bug fixes|

 An API product can consist of multiple individual deployable packages. e.g. An API product consists of the API implementation (code and interface specification) but can also come with a SDK supporting different languages. To keep a flexible and consistent versioning scheme across the different individual deployable packages, the following versioning rules are defined:

Versioning rules:

* The API Product must dictate the MAJOR version number.

* Individual deployable packages part of the API product all follow the MAJOR version but can differ on MINOR and PATCH version level.
* MAJOR versions can run in parallel; MINOR and PATCH not.

Also, following the API Lifecycle rules with versioning. When a new MAJOR version is deployed, the previous version should be Deprecated.

How to apply versioning to our APIs is defined in our Engineering Baseline:

* Web API versioning standard
* Event API versioning standard

Business API Design Checklist

Document that outlines key questions and tasks to address as part of Business API design work.

The checklist questions provides the starting point for addressing business need and are applied in an iterative approach.

In practice the checklist can be used:

* as agenda points to facilitate architecture/engineering/design conversations and workshops related to Business APIs
* to structure the documentation/communication related to a Business API
* for communication and alignment on how enterprise capability and data models that impacts the design of Business APIs
* as governance checklist when reviewing a designed solution from an architecture/engineering perspective

1. Understand Business Need

Get an understanding of specific business need and context connected to the specific solution design scope.

*Key roles involved*: Technology Architect, Software Engineer, Data Architect, Product Owner, Roadmap Leader, DX Designer

* What are the key use cases and business capabilities that business software for this solution design scope must be able to support?
* What are the measurable outcomes, user experiences and time to market that this solution is targeting?
* Is there any strategic business need that should be considered that is not part of direct business need input?
* Is the solution targeting all or some markets? Can market specific requirements and capabilities be expected?
* What are the enterprise level business capability, process and information models that are relevant to this specific solution scope (such as IBCM)?

2. Determine needed Software Capabilities in Ingka context

Understand how this solution scope fits into the context of the Ingka digital ecosystem and the ambition of building distinct, reusable software capabilities.

*Key roles involved*: Technology Architect, Software Engineer, Data Architect

* What capabilities are covered by the enterprise level Architecture Area software capability model?
* If need for designing new AA Software Capabilities, what existing Ingka level Data Concepts and Ingka Data Models are related to this solution scope?
* Are there any established modernization plans connected to software capabilities and systems within this solution scope?

3. Determine needed Business API capabilities

Determine the unique and disctinct software capability that the Business API should provide.

*Key roles involved*: Technology Architect, Software Engineer, Data Architect

* What are the Business APIs that are needed by this solution on abstract/logical level? ("Business API first")
* What types of Business API styles and integration patterns are feasible from a consumer perspective?
* Is there a need for considering future API consumers from an omnichannel perspective?
* What Logical Business APIs exist as part of the Architecture Area model for this solution scope?
* If need for designing new Logical Business APIs, what are the Architecture Area capabilities and Data Concepts that provides the base for naming APIs?

4. Design Business API specification

*Key roles involved*: Software Engineer, Technology Architect, Data Architect

* What Logical Business APIs and Data Concepts are the starting points for the design of the actual Business API specification?
* For Web API and Event API, what "root level" Data Entity schema elements are targeted in payload structure and/or API URI path?
* Are there any reusable, canonical Data Entity models on Ingka level that should be considered? Schema Object Library? Schema Registry?
* What existing APIs are representing data close to this solution scope?
* Is the API specification aligned with the Ingka API standards?

5. Implement Business APIs

*Key roles involved*: Software Engineer, Technology Architect, Data Architect

* What enterprise and domain level technology standards need to be considered when implementing the Business API specification?
* How has NFRs related to performance, scalability and reliability been considered?

Business API Design Principles

This document captures design principles that guides Business API development and also provides the base for establishing measurements and governance of Business API quality and progress.

§ A Business API must represent a distinct capability in context of enterprise architecture and Ingka Retail Platform

A key design goal is to develop Business APIs that represent distinct and unique capability "building blocks" as part of an Ingka Retail Platform.

The approach for achieving this is to **establish a Business API design process that actively considers the enterprise level Architecture Area model** in terms of designing the entprise level "ubiquitous language" for "Software Capabilities and targeted Logical Business APIs.

For an introduction to the Business API design approach and activities, see the *How do you design a Business API* section in FAQ - What does Business API mean to Software Engineering? for more information.

For an overview of the current state of working methods and tools used in Business API design, see FAQ - What are the models, repositories and tools that is used for designing a Business API?.

§ A Business API Specification must be designed to be independent and have a longer lifetime than any specific software system implementation

A key enterprise architecture goal is the separation of the Business API specification from the specific implementation in one or more software systems.

The key motivation behind this separation is captured in the illustration below:

* Ability to replace/decommission systems with minimum impact on existing integrations (aka "loose coupling")
* Ability to support multiple implementation of the same Business API
* Ability to implement market specific variations of global standard APIs

!Business API are System independent

*Example*: A Business API for Find Customer can remain the same even if the system implementation changes from inhouse to vendor to inhouse software.

The implication of this principle is captured by a number of rules.

Rule: A Business API Specification is designed using system independent and industry standard API schema technologies

Using API Specification standards such as *Open API* for Business Web APIs or *Asynch API*.

For Business Data APIs the Ingka standard for *Data Product* development will set the standard that today is based BigQuery database schema.

Rule: The Business API Specification and documentation should be managed outside of any specific software system

Meaning that the Business API Specification from a source code perspective is managed within a namespace and github repository that represent enterprise level API structure rather than within a system repository.

Meaning that the backlog and lifecycle of a Business API is managed separately from the individual software system backlog.

§ A Business API Specification must be based on consistent and standard Data representation

A key enterprise architecture ambition is that Business APIs represent data consistently and are algined with the established Ingka level data models.

The expected benefits of a consistent data representation include:

* **Enhanced Data Quality and Accuracy**.  A standardized data representation establishes a common language for data across the organization. This uniformity eliminates ambiguity and ensures that data is consistently interpreted and extracted. As a result, data quality improves, reducing the risk of errors and inconsistencies.

* **Improved Data Integration and Interoperability**. A standardized data representation facilitates seamless integration and interoperability between different systems and applications.

* **Reduced Development Costs and Time to Market**.  Standardized data representation simplifies application development and integration. Developers can focus on core business logic rather than wasting time on data format conversions or error handling. This leads to faster development cycles and reduced costs.

* **Enhanced Data Security and Compliance**. Standardized data representation can contribute to improved data security and compliance.
By clearly defining data types, structures, and access controls, organizations can better protect sensitive information and adhere to data privacy regulations.

Rule: A Business API name and schema elements must be aligned with enterprise level Data Concept and Data Entity models

Established Data Concepts can be found in Ingka Data Model and Data Concept Glossary (part of Data Catalog).

The Schema Object Library proof of concept contains examples on Data Entity reuse based on Open API Domain schemas.

*Example*: Data Concepts such as Customer, Sales Order and Retail Item are used in the "bounded context" of many Business APIs

Rule: A Business API Specification should be designed to internally enable reuse of data representation across complex schema objects and API operations

Within an API schema the same data representation in terms of object, attribute and enumeration is typically reused across different operations. The architecture ambition is to minimize the number of duplicate data representations within the same API specification.

In context of a an Open API or Aych API specification it means separating reusable data representation into the separate "components: schema:" section.

*Example*: Using independent component schema objects in Open API and Asynch API Specifications

```yaml
openapi: 3.0.0
info:
  title: Business Customer Master
...
   get:
      responses:
        "200":
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/BusinessCustomer"
          description: The full business customer entry
...

components:
  schemas:
    BusinessCustomer:
      type: object
      additionalProperties: false
      description: The full business customer entry
      properties:
        id:
          type: string
          format: uuid
        sourceLastUpdatedDate:
          type: string
          format: date-time
        industrySegment:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/IndustrySegmentEnum"
        name:
          type: string
          maxLength: 255
        customerCategory:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/CustomerCategoryEnum"
... 
```

Rule: A Business API Specification should make intentional decisions on the use strongly typed data representation

Overall, strongly typed data is generally recommended for API specifications to enhance data accuracy, developer productivity, and testing capabilities.

However, it's essential to balance strong typing with flexibility to avoid excessive rigidity and impact the API adaptability over time.

Strongly typed data within an API specification provides several advantages (according to Bard AI wisdom):

* Improved Data Accuracy: Strong typing enforces data format validation and prevents invalid or unexpected data from being processed. This reduces the risk of errors and ensures data integrity.
* Enhanced Developer Productivity: Strong typing provides clear and unambiguous data structures, making it easier for developers to understand and interact with the API. This simplifies code development and reduces the likelihood of syntax errors.
* Simplified Testing and Validation: Strong typing facilitates automated testing and validation, allowing developers to check for data consistency and adherence to API specifications. This streamlines the development process and ensures code quality.

* Reduced Deployment Risks: Strong typing reduces the likelihood of introducing errors during API deployment, as it enforces data format consistency across development, testing, and production environments. This enhances reliability and reduces the risk of unexpected failures.
* Enhanced API Usability: Strong typing can improve the usability of an API by providing clear and consistent data definitions. This makes it easier for developers to integrate the API into their applications and reduces the risk of misinterpretation or misuse.

However, excessive strong typing can also introduce drawbacks:

* Reduced Flexibility: Strong typing can make it more difficult to accommodate changes in data structures or requirements. This can limit the API's adaptability and hinder its ability to evolve over time.
* Increased Developer Learning Curve: Developers may need to learn more about strong typing and its implications, which can initially increase the learning curve for API adoption.
* Potential Performance Impact: In some cases, strong typing can introduce minor performance overhead, as data validation and type conversion may incur additional processing steps.

*Example*: Strongly typed Data representation in an Open API Specification

```yaml
    ContactMethod:
      type: object
      description: Contact Method data for a given business customer and contact method id
      properties:
        id:
          type: string
          format: uuid
        type:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/ContactMethodTypeEnum"
        verificationStatus:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/ContactMethodVerificationStatusEnum"
        verificationSource:
          type: string
          nullable: true
          maxLength: 255
        verificationDate:
          type: string
          nullable: true
          format: date-time
        value:
          type: string
          maxLength: 255
...
    ContactMethodVerificationStatusEnum:
      type: string
      default: NOT_VERIFIED
      enum:
        - VERIFIED
        - NOT_VERIFIED
        - INVALID

```

§ A Business API must be designed and implemented based on enterprise technology standards and guidelines

Motivation is to achieve ambitions of low costs, scalability, monitoring, response time performance and reduced modernization/refactoring efforts.

Examples of enterprise level technology standards:

* Ingka Retail Platform
* Ingka Engineering Baseline

The implication of this overall principle is detailed by a number of rules.

Rule: A Business API must follow enterprise design guidelines for specific API Styles

A Business API is based on different API Styles and addition to comply with Business API specific rules, the design must consider Ingka level standards defined for a particular API Style:

* Web API Standards
* Event API Standards

Rule: A Business API must have a design and implementation that has actively considered NFR needs and CAP theorem

A Business API implementation need to apply the layering patterns of the Ingka Retail Platform.

Design documentation has explicit information on need and considerations related to Non Functional Requirements such as scalability, monitoring and response time performance.

Design documents reasoning on how to balance implementation across Consistency, Availability and Partition tolerance aspects.

§ A Business API must be published and discoverable using an enterprise level API Management portal

A key aspect of Business APIs is the ability to explore, test and initiate the use of existing Business APIs.

Rule: Business Web APIs and Event APIs are published in Allen API Catalog

Allen portal

Rule: Business Data APIs are published in Data Catalog implemented by Data Products

Data Catalog
Business API Design Guidelines

Introduction

The intention of this document is to "demystify" the strategic ambition of developing Business APIs and what it means to software engineering and API design.

In practice a Business API represents a well designed, enterprise level API that is developed in line with the strategic Ingka Retail Platform (IRP) ambitions of achieving flexibility in the digital ecosystem:

* Business API specifications are designed independent from individual system implementations
* Business API design is aligned with enterprise level software capability and data models to ensure distinct and consistent APIs and data representation
* Business API implementation and publication is aligned with specific enterprise guidelines, patterns and technology standards

What is an "API"? According to AI wisdom *"an API (Application Programming Interface) is a set of rules and specifications that define how two pieces of software can communicate with each other. It acts as an intermediary, allowing different applications to exchange data and functionality without needing to know the inner workings of each other"*. Given the current industry focus on RESTful Web APIs, it is important to note that not APIs comes in many different styles.
In the case of a Business API, it can be developed using the API Style of Web API, Event API or Data API. (See API Classifications for more on API styles and categorizations).

Solution Design

How do you design an API? From a general perspective the design of APIs is an iterative process that typically involves the activities illustrated in the diagram below.

1. Initial solutioning of involved software and dependencies (aka "Conceptual software modelling")
  1.1 Using a mix of conceptual and established names of systems, capabilities, data and APIs

2. Designing ideal target software boundaries and integrations (aka "Logical software modelling")
   2.1 Aligning and extending the standard "ubiquitous language" of software capabilities and data representation terms/concepts used for the solution scope
   2.2 Designing the ideal separation of software boundaries, data dependencies and integrations (aka "logical model")

3.

Making initial decision and assumptions on APIs and integration patterns
   3.1 Designing actual System boundaries, APIs and Integrations (aka "Physical software modelling")
   3.1 Creating one or more solution views with actual systems, APIs and integrations

!General overview of API design process

Note that depending on solution complexity and maturity of the solution space, the activity (2) *Designing ideal software boundaries and dependencies* can be implicit or explicit. For example when it clear from the start what actual systems and APIs that will collaborate in the solution.

Solution Design in Ingka Retail Platform context

Given Ingka strategic ambitions of reducing point-to-point integrations between systems and provide flexibility in a larger digital ecosystem, there is a need to apply API design thinking that does not directly start on system level.
A main purpose of Business APIs and Ingka Retail Platform is to apply "Business API First" thinking that actively considers the strategic ambitions of creating distinct, reusable Business APIs that represent functional building blocks that can be reused across solutions.
The illustration below uses a fictive example to that gives a simplified view of the target "Business API First" design process that will be further elaborated in this document.

!"Business API First" design process

Main difference from the general API design process is the specific use of Ingka enterprise level architecture models when determining the ideal, logical software capabilties and data representation:

* the **Architecture Area Model** covering the enterprise level structure of software capabilties
* the **Ingka Data Model** covering the core data abstractions to be used when designing data representation in context of API schemas, Database schemas and Code.
For a more detailed introduction to Business APIs from software enginnering perspective, see the FAQ - What does Business API mean to Software Engineering? confluence page.

What is a Business API?

See Businsess API Concept that covers an introduction to the purpose, definition and characteristics of the Business API concept.

How do you design and implement a Business API?

The design of a Business API is triggered by any business development activity that identifies the need for a distinct software capability that is to be provided using a published API.

The following diagram provides an overview of the activities involved in designing a Business API and the relationship to the enterprise level Architecture Area software capability model.

!Business API Design Overview

Note! Consider roles involved as preliminary and input to work on defining solution design process.

Key messages in diagram:

* **Understand Business Need** activity that includes analysis of specific requirements as well as the context of the IKEA Business Capability Model
  * *Product Owner* is responsible for the activity, supported by PEDX, Roadmap and Architecture roles
* **Conceptual Solution Design** activity that provides the initial brainstorming and solutioning of involved software and dependencies
  * *Lead Software Engineer* (or "leading Software Engineer") is responsible,

supported by roles across PEDX and Architecture
* **Design Software Capabilties and needed Business APIs in enterprise context** activity is done based on the Architecture Area Model and the Ingka Data Model
  * *Technology Architect* is responsible, supported by Engineer and Data Architecture roles
* **Design Business API** activity including the naming of actual Business API specification, operations and schema elements are based on the AA Logical Business APIs and any existing standard/canonical Data Entity models
  * *Lead Engineer* is responsible, supported by Technology Architect and Data Architect
* **Implement Business API** activity based on software layering patterns and technology standards connected to Ingka Retail Platform
  * *Software Engineer* is responsible

The Demystifying Business API presentation provides a more detailed introduction to the Business API Design process including content examples.

What are the design principles that guides Business API development?

See Business API Design Principles

What are key questions and tasks to address as part of Business API design work?

See Business API Design Checklist.

How do you name a Business API?

For naming of a Logical Business API, see this page.

For naming of actual Business APIs, see Ingka API standards that links to:

* Business Web API - Naming Standard  
* Business Event API - Naming Standard

In addition to naming of the API itself, there are also naming standards for individual API schema elements on Data Entity and Attribute level:

* Naming standards for Data Concepts, Data Entity and Data Entity Attribute

How do you design a Business API in vendor platform context?

From a Business API design perspective, an in-house development (aka "Make") is a straight forward scenario where the design and implementation of software capabilities and Business APIs goes hand-in-hand.

However, in a "Buy" vendor software scenario the Business API design can use the following different approaches:

1. Promote the vendor API to be the Business API for a particular capability
2. Design a new Business API that works as a adapter towards the vendor API
  2.A Adapter software is developed within the boundary of the vendor system
  2.B Adapter software is developed as part of a separate system boundary  

Decision for what approach depends on vendor software opportunities and Ingka integration design patterns.

For more details, see Interoperatibility Pattern

How do you determine the Business API Compliance for a published API?

Not all published Ingka APIs in Allen portal can be considered as Business APIs that live up to Business API design principles.

In order to measure progress, an *Business API Compliance Indicator* has been introduced to be able to measure overall progress and quality of Business API development.

For details on the current approach for measuring Business API Compliance, see FAQ - How do you measure Business API Compliance for a published API?

For details on the target thinking for measuring IRP and Business API progress, see Ingka Retail Platform - Metrics

Event Design

These guidelines apply to creating Event based Business API's. Although they are also encouraged to be used for System API's as well.

Business API's are used to communicate between Systems that are located in different Bounded Contexts.

These guidelines make use of concepts defined in the Domain Driven Design community that provide a good theoretical background on designing entities that fit well into an event driven approach.

See the following links for more information about these concepts:

- Commands & Events
- Aggregates, Entities & Value-objects
- Bounded Contexts & Domains

MUST: Consider Event based caching as well as just-in-time queries

Although asynchronous message processing is preferred, do not blindly apply Event publishing and consumption as the silver-bullet integration pattern for all use cases.

*Rationale*

Event based caching into a consuming system can also lead to complexity and overengineering. We continuously need to consider the just-in-time query counterpart as well.

Consider the following:

**Reasons for just-in-time queries:**

- dependency on availability, response time & throughput of the producing service is not a concern. A query API could then be a viable and simpler solution.

**Reasons for event-based caching:**

- **Sparsely needing data of object instances**
  - e.g. you need data from a small percentage of the object instances.
  - e.g. you need additional data about the customer in some exceptional scenario's. Instead of caching a lot of PII sensitive data in the consuming service, it will do a REST based query and only keep this information in memory, thereby lowering the risk on a data breach.

- **Complex query with limited resulting data**
  - e.g. you need a count of the amount of objects that conform to a relative large set of query parameters.
  - e.g. the amount of orders that are planned for a certain timeslot with a certain transport provider.
    - Instead of consuming a lot of event types that deals with the whole lifecycle of Work Order creating, scheduling, planning & deviation handling, you only need to do a simple API query.

Note: query messages can be implemented synchronously, but also asynchronously.

SHOULD : Create events for every Aggregate that you want to expose outside your context

Whenever a service provides read access to some Aggregate for services from other contexts, it should also provide an event stream for this Aggregate.

*Rationale*

To reduce the number of synchronous GET calls, a service should subscribe to event streams to maintain a local representation of data owned by another service. The
information provided by entities owned by a service should be completely recoverable from the appropriate event streams.

It does not have to cover the full schema of the aggregate, just the parts that are needed outside the context. This is linked to the more general topic of reducing dependencies across contexts.

The same general design rules apply to both REST endpoints and events. The Commands that modify an Aggregate should result in appropriate events that describe the modifications.

SHOULD: Use domain specific events

Instead of using CRUD events like entity-created, entity-updated, entity-deleted you should use custom events that refer to the entity's domain.

*Rationale*

Consumers that want to react to a domain specific state transition can do this much better if they are not required to infer the transition based on the payloads attributes. Some consumers might even be able to not persist the payload or to skip certain events. This will lead to a more efficient overall system.

- 👎customer-created

- 👍customer-registered

SHOULD: Document state transition diagrams for each Aggregate

Business Events reflect the evolution of an object that is relevant to the business. It should be clear which transitions can occur, what the semantics is of the Aggregate being in a certain state, and when and why it can transition to a next state.

A logical delete can be the point in time where the Aggregate has reached an end state where it will become immutable for the normal Business Process flow. e.g. "OrderCompleted".

*Rationale*

This makes it clear for the subscriber to which events it should subscribe and which ones it should ignore. Also, it makes clear at what point a consumer can stop to track a certain object for its own purposes and clean up it internal state, or move it to a cheaper storage tier and keep it for analytical purposes.

MUST: Only represent changes that already happened and are persisted

An event may only be written to the messaging infrastructure once the transaction persisting the state change has successfully completed.

*Rationale*

Events are meant to distribute information about what changed in the persistent state of a service. Thus, they may only be published after the change.

MUST: Keep events self-contained

All information necessary to "understand" an event must be contained in the event.

All information about the state of the entity after applying the change described by the event must be contained in the event.

The preferred way to achieve this is by including at least the information that changed in the Aggregate as result of the Command that was executed.

*Rationale*

A consumer ideally should have no need to make calls back to the event producing system while processing an event (barring the exceptions mentioned at the top of the page).

MUST : Events link to the Aggregate scope and are owned by a single Aggregate

Even if events communicating a relevant change that happens on an underlying Entity of an Aggregate, it is still linked to the owning Aggregate.

This can also be mirrored to a REST interface: If a resource has sub resources, the events should be associated with the root resource. Thus, even if just a property of a sub resource changes, an event for the whole aggregation should be published.

*Example: A service maintaining a shopping basket allows read access via a REST call. A basket (the Root Entity of the Aggregate) consists of some general attributes and a list of line items. Additionally, a client can create a basket, add / remove line items and delete a basket. Thus, the service should provide an event stream for baskets. The events should contain all information that is accessible via the Business API REST interface. After creation of a basket, a "basket-created" event should be published. After deletion, a "basket-deleted" should be published.

And, depending on the detailed design of modification interactions, the service should for example publish "basket-line-item-added" and "basket-line-item-removed" events upon modification. And those line item events will identify the basket that they are part of.*

*Rationale*

The transactional completeness of the Root entity can't be guaranteed if it does not conceptually contains its sub resources.

Looking at the basket example again, there should be only a basket topic and no basket line item topic. Even if just the quantity of a single line item changes, the event for the basket must be published.

SHOULD: Keep events focused

The different changes of an entity should not be consolidated into *just one* event type. There should be one focused event for each type of change that is relevant to the flow of business processes.

*Rationale*

Consumers that want to react to a domain specific state transition can do this much better if they are not required to infer the transition based on the payloads attributes. Some consumers might even be able to not persist the payload or to skip certain events. This will lead to a more efficient overall system.

- 👎
  - basket-updated (converying all events mentioned below)

- 👍
  - basket-created
  - basket-changed
  - basket-cleared
  - basket-abandoned
  - basket-line-item-added
  - basket-line-item-removed

*Note: multiple events can be raised as the result of a single command. And you can have root level events emitted at the same time as detail events. E.g. think about creating a basket, then adding 10 items and then clearing it.
The "clear" command would result into 11 events being emitted. 1 for the basket, 10 for each line-item.*

MUST: Name events after the Aggregate Root entity and the change that happened

An event must be named with pattern <root-entity>-<name of change>, e.g. basket-created.

*Rationale*

As every event is associated with a specific entity, the event should be named after this entity.

As events describe a change that has already happened, this change should also be included in the name.

As the event already should have happened, the change should be named in past tense.

Events associated with baskets should be named something like:

- basket-created
- basket-deleted
- basket-line-item-added
- basket-line-item-removed
- basket-line-item-quantity-changed

MUST: Exclude mutable or sensitive data from linked resources that are outside the scope of the Aggregate

Related resources that might be embedded in your REST response should not be included in your event payload.

It is a good practice to only publish the ID / candidate keys / address of a linked resource instead of all of its attributes. Also, it is a good practice to include the sequence number to indicate the point in time state that was relevant at the time of the event of the linked resource.

Immutable attributes may be included as well. Mutable attributes should only be included if they are "frozen" for the rest of the linked resource's lifecycle.

Examples of immutable attributes: Candidate keys (such as IDs in external systems, human readable names that increase analysability)

*Rationale*

Including mutable data in your topic forces you to send updates whenever an embedded entity changes. This can lead to a mass of updates, for example when many updates happen at once and many services embed data like this.

Guidance

This section contains longer guides and tutorials related to IRP.

Each guide comes as its own page and helps the reader understand how to do a particular task, such as designing a Business API in a standardised way.

This section will grow as needed and will be influenced by questions being asked and to clarify specific aspects of IRP.

Getting Started

Designing and building a product to fit in with the IRP requires that we come to a clear set of boundaries and getting the right stakeholders involved.

!Solution Design Overview

As illustrated above, the Solution Design work can be divided into three main solution design activities:

- **Initial Solution Design** that makes a first assessment of scope, feasibility and clarity on products/systems involved
- **Solution High Level Design** that focus on interactions and software capabilities across several products/systems and/or domains
- **Individual System/Product Design** that focus on the APIs, Integrations and internal structure of a specific Product/System that is contributing within the solution scope

As we are applying the principles of Domain Driven Design (DDD) to our landscape, these phases can also be aligned to different types of activities within the DDD context.

| Activity | DDD Level | DDD Workshops & Techniques |
|----------|--------------|---------------|
| Initial Solution Design | Strategic Design | Big Picture Event Storming |
| Solution High Level Design | Tactical Design | Design Level Event Storming |
| Individual System/Product Design | Software Design | Software Architecture Patterns & Onion Architecture |

Check out the DDD Crew's Open Source practices for a lot of these techniques.

Minimal architectural foundation

The above activities help to establish the right foundation to ensure that the products designed will be composable across different business processes and use cases.

It is important to validate that the following things are in place before working on a solution that will adapt existing products & APIs or create new ones.

Initial Solution Design

Check the following:

- What are the business needs & goals?
  - What are key business capabilities that need to be supported / unlocked to be succesful?
  - What end-to-end process is being supported / changed?
  - What are key non-functional requirements that need to be supported to be successful?
- Impacted Architecture Areas are clear

Solution High Level Design

Check the following:

- Impacted Bounded Contexts are clear
- Impacted Systems are clear
  - What impacted capabilities are assigned to which system?
  - What is the information flow through the system landscape?
  - Which system owns the Data Concepts that are impacted?

Individual System / Product Design

Check the following:

- Data Concept internals are clear
  - What entities / schema structure makes up the Data Concept?
- System Responsibilities are clear
  - What APIs are offered by which system?
  - Which systems are consuming the APIs?

If above questions cannot be clearly answered, design activities should be executed to get this clarity.
Also, all PDEX and architecture stakeholders need to be aligned on these results so that we ensure that we are working towards a common goal.

Design Guidance

As part of the guidance, we highlight a number of important design activities and detail further how they work.

| Level | Activity | PDK Guidance | External Guidance |
|-------|----------|--------------|-------------------|
| Strategic / Tactical / Software| Event Storming | | Event Storming Cheat Sheet|
| Strategic | Context Mapping | | Context Map Patterns|

The Ingka Retail Platform (IRP) and Platform Developer Kit (PDK)

What is the Ingka Retail Platform? (IRP)

The technology, products, and services developed by our teams play an important role in the success of IKEA to stay *relevant and affordable*. However, aligning diverse teams and initiatives is challenging due to the intricacies and dependencies within our work.

**The Ingka Retail Platform (IRP) concept** redefines our approach to how we work to encourage the use and reuse of solutions. This empowers teams to enhance, scale, and build upon existing solutions, facilitating **quicker, high-quality, and cost-effective delivery of business value**.

The IRP will allow us to create open business models and platforms that enable our teams to scale extremely fast with minimal investments. Also, it will improve the quality of our work so to delight our customers and co-workers with **intentional** unique IKEA experiences at the right time and place. We need to adapt to this new way of working to sustain **profitable growth and to stay financially fit** and we expect all of you to contribute to this new way of working.

The IRP concept consist of multiple viewpoints that work together:

- **Meta model & Taxonomy**: The meta model describes all concepts as a ubiquitous language within the IRP itself.
  - This enables us to apply a consistent terminology across the various parts of the PDK and defining the IRP itself.
- **Reference Architecture**: A reference architecture that is foundational to the way we structure or systems and teams
  - This defines the form and type of "Lego blocks" and ways of connecting them
- **Way of Working** : A set of activities to execute in collaboration so that we design these integrated capabilities with a good balance between autonomy and integration complexity
  - This defines how various stakeholders work together to design and build the digital products
- **Composable set of Digital Products** : A concrete set of building blocks that conforms to the reference architecture to offer the capabilities needed to execute our business processes
  - This defines the actual "Lego blocks" that we can work with

This **platform developer kit (PDK)** focuses on the *Reference Architecture* and the *Way of Working*.
And by applying this, the outcome are the *Composable Digital Products*.
Platform Developer Kit

What is a Platform Developer Kit?

This documentation contains a Platform Developer Kit focused on Ingka Retail Platform. It takes inspiration from how SDK/PDK documentation is structured in the industry, with the ambition to provide guidance for engineering on how to develop specifically for IRP.

What is this Platform Developer Kit covering?

This PDK will help you understand more about the architecture direction for our system landscape and find examples, references, standards and enabling tools and platforms that will help you build systems in accordance to the reference architecture.

Just like you get from any SDK/PDK documentation, you will find an overview, reference patterns, guides & tutorials, standards, code samples and tools which supports you to design and develop software in line with the IRP Reference Architecture.

!IRP PDK

Reference Architecture Overview

The IRP-RA is an enterprise level reference architecture. It is split into different layers based on Non-Functional Requirements. Each layer has a specific purpose and with that they require different architecture approaches.

We split the architecture into layers to improve scalability and availability, and still be able to keep consistency. A System can span more than one layer, but a software component within a system are within a single layer.
The layers and systems are supported by Technology & Engineering platforms that helps teams solve common, commodity problems.

Patterns that are commonly applied within the Ingka Retail Platform are described in the Reference Patterns.

More explanation about the layers below can be found in the Concepts section: Systems of Record & Systems of Truth.

!Layers

!!! Note
    For more information about Ingka Retail Platform see the EA Confluence page. There you will find more on the business context, background, benefits, details on the layers and the goals and metrics we have set for the platform.

Meta-model & Taxonomy

In order to communicate guidance in a consistent way, it is important that we apply a consistent terminology within the architecture landscape as well.

Good work has already been done as part of the Enterprise Meta Model, Software Boundary Classification and Software Design Abstraction Levels.

Unification of terminology

Not al terminology aligns across the mentioned sources above. Also, the Enterprise Meta Model aims to cover everything in the enterprise, while for IRP we want to focus on the parts that will influence architecture and design choices. Therefore we have created a focused model that unifies the different sources into a single terminology.

Linking well-known frameworks

Since Ingka co-workers within Digital come from various backgrounds, they will have different industry-wide frameworks and taxonomies as a reference point. With the IRP Architecture Meta Model, we show the definition of the terms and also link it to other frameworks, so it is clear how they align if you come from a different background.

Way-of-working

In order to realize composable digital products, we need to work together across all digital disciplines: Product, Data, Engineering, UX, Architecture, and Security.

To keep a consistent focus we need to establish and align the boundaries of business capabilities, mapped to software systems and the APIs they provide for composability. Depending on the scope, different stakeholders will be involved to shape these boundaries.

!IRP Collaboration

Since several layers need to be aligned, it is important to define which parties are involved with what parts. So that we all can work towards a common model and know who to contact to keep evolving the IRP.

Therefore there is a matrix that highlights who is involved in what areas from all of the relevant roles.

The Guidance section describes the activities to be executed to get the alignment described above.
Caching Strategy, Patterns and Anti-Patterns

Introduction

In this section you will learn about the following

* What is Caching
* Layers where Caching can be enabled
* Caching strategies
* Cache in the CQRS Design Pattern
* Cache and the CAP Theorem
* Invalidating the Cache
* How it supports our Architecture Strategy
* How Caching works in Ingka Retail Platform
* When to use this pattern and when **not** to use it.
* Open Items

Overview

Caching is the strategy commonly used to help reduce the latency of the application and give the users a quick response to their queries. When used correctly it helps in giving the users a quick and consistent experience of the application. However, if wrongly used it could lead to cause confusion with stale, inconsistent data and also lead to poor performance of the application.

The purpose of this note is to discuss the various caching layers, common patterns used for caching, caching best practices and look scenarios that warrant the use of a Cache/Caching Service.

Also provide guidance on how caching is intended to work as it will be in each of the layer in IRP (Ingka Retail Platform).
This pattern aims at helping Systems achieve quicker response times and improving the availability and resilience of the systems.

It supports a number of EA Principles that we have defined on the corporate level (see the link for the rationale behind these principles):

* Responsive
* Resilience and scalability
* Build for horizontal scaling

Above aspects are also nicely explained in the Reactive Manifesto, explaining some more about the rationale.

Use Cases

Why Cache : Good use cases are when:

* Heavy computations are to be done
* Multiple database lookups or joins are involved in fetching the results
* The system state does not change very frequently
* You have a read heavy system that fetches the same data repeatedly
* Frequently access information, pictures or videos
* Reduce the latency
* Availability is of higher priority than consistency
* You can deal with eventual consistency across (sub)systems

Non-use cases are when:

* You have a write intensive system which changes the system state frequently
* Consistency is of higher priority and you don't want to delay the writes into the system

Cache -  Benefits

* Improved response time
* Increased scalability
* Reduced network trafficca
* Improved reliability
* Cost Savings
* Better use of resources
* Offload & Reduce Load on Backend
Cache -  Layers

Local Browser Cache

!Local Browser Cache

One of the quickest and easiest form of caching is using the local cache of the user. The data is held on the client side (browser) using key-value pairs. In the case of Web Applications, the application can use the storage available on the Users (Client-side) system. Using Web Storage APIs the application can persist large amounts of data without impacting the website performance. Unlike cookies this has higher storage capacity and the information is not passed back to the server side. Web storage is stored per origin and therefore accessible for all pages from the same origin.

You could use localStorage or sessionStorage to persist the data, however, note that the session storage is live only for the duration of the active session.

Since this is browser based, the browsers capability to support Web Storage should be checked as well and this can also be done using the Web storage APIs. Also look out for issues when using objects, for storing properties, that are likely to be inherited from other objects.

Benefit of local caching is that is easy to use and is supported by all modern web browsers.

Since it is using the client-side cache there is no worry about the adding more resources to handle the space requirements.

The benefit of using local cache is however lost if the user uses different browsers or different machines to access the same web app. Also ensure that data that is likely to change on the server side should not be persisted on the client side as there is no mechanism to proactively invalidate keys in the local storage.

Caching resources on the Client Side

Fetching resources from the server is always a costly call. Resources like client-side scripts, static documents, stylesheets, images, videos can always be cached on the client side. The browser typically checks the HTTP Header to see what resources to cache. The browser uses this information to check with the server if the resource(s) is stale, after the max-age specified in the initial response has passed and based on the servers response gets the new resource from the server or uses the one that is currently in the local cache.
This usually leads to issues with clients that do not refresh their browsers for a longer duration and then decides to continue with the activity at a later point in time or when the client has resources downloaded and are returning to the website after sometime but the resource max-age is not expired. In such scenarios there are various cache invalidation techniques that could be adopted.

Cache Busting

Ways to get the client to get the latest resources from the server and not continue using the stale ones downloaded

* Versioning the file - giving the new resource a different version which makes the browser download the resource as a new one
* Fingerprinting - adding a fingerprint to the resource
* Appending query string - adding a query string to the end of the filename. This should be used carefully as certain proxy servers do not allow caching of the resources if they are accompanied with a query string.
Best Practices

Do

* Use the Cache-Control and ETag headers to control cache behavior for static assets
* Set long max-age values to reap the benefits of browser cache
* Use fingerprinting or versioning for cache busting

Don’t

* Use HTML meta tags to specify cache behavior
* Use query strings for cache busting

Edge Cache

!Edge Cache

Read this page to get a view of how caching has been implemented on IKEA.com

API Gateway Cache

!API Gateway

We can enable caching at the API Gateway. The configuration varies based on the Gateway being used but essentially the parameters to look for in the API Gateway while caching would be the TTL (Time To Live), Cache Capacity, encryption requirement and authorization.

The response can be cached on the Query Parameters, Path parameters or the Headers in the request.

URL and a unique cache key are important for API caching

Cache in Data & Services (In context of SoT in IRP)

Caching in SoT, often referred to as service caching or data caching, is a used to improve performance of microservices and digital systems. It involves temporarily storing frequently accessed or computed data in a cache,  to reduce the need to fetch or compute the same data repeatedly from the SoR.

Cache in Core Systems  (In context of SoR in IRP)

Caching in SoR or "caching in database systems," involves the use of caching techniques to optimize the performance and responsiveness of applications that rely on data stored in databases or other systems of record.

Caching Pattern Walkthrough

* The Event Publisher notifies of a change in the system state
* The consumers read the changes
* Systems of Record persists the data and further publishes the changes to its state
* The event is published for all interested parties
* System of Truth will pick up these events, run business logic or do service aggregation to enhance the data and publish them.
* System of Truth will persist the data to the cache
* Systems interested in the events will consume the changes

IRP Context

!How

Scenario 1

Important: There should be a way to invalidate the cache from SoR in this example

* The Channels publishes the details of the change to the Event Stream
* The systems of record that are subscribed pick up the command
* The system of record processes the command and could further publish a different event
* The system of truth picks up the command
* System of Truth makes the model available to the Channels as further commands through the Business APIs
* Channels could also consume the events that the Systems of Record has published
* Channel can cache the information retrieved

!CQRS

Scenario 2

* The Channel invokes the ReST API to persist the information
* System of Truth can cache the information and make the information available to System of Record
* System of Record processes the Event and persists the data
* System of Record makes the information queries by the Channels published for the Consumers
* System of Truth will cache the information published by the System of Record in the models required by the Channels (or Consumers)
* Channels or consumers can fetch the cached information from the Systems of Truth through the Business APIs

!Scenario2

Cache -  CAP Theorem

CAP (Consistency, Availability and Partition Tolerance) theorem states that in a distributed system it is not possible to provide all the desirable properties at the same time.

Therefore it is always a tight line to either make the data Available or Consistent in a distributed solution.

In distributed caching one of the common questions to ask while selecting a Caching strategy would be, should the Cache be able to respond to the requests irrespective of the Backend systems being available or should the Cache always serve the right and consistent data.

Cache -  CQRS Design Pattern

CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) is a pattern that separates the need for getting information (query) and the actions that could change the state of a system (command). A query does not mutate the state of the system unlike a command.

The query part usually has no business logic associated with the read and is a straight-forward retrieval of the current state of the system.

Caching can be implemented on the read side. In this case the Application is responsible for persisting the data to the persistent store and then generating the read-models.

!CQRS

For more information on CQRS pattern please visit
Caching Strategies

Read-aside Caching

!Read Aside Cache

This caching mechanism uses lazy-loading, the data in the cache serves the requests and the application is responsible for fetching the data if the data in the cache does not exist.

This pattern goes with the assumption that the data is cached based on a request for the data at least once and so will be requested again.

Usually the first fetch is costly as it needs to fetch data from the persistent store, however, this is usually offset by the developers pre-warming the cache with frequently accessed queries.

An example: In the Selling Domain, there is a need for various products to access the Range, Stock and Availability. These details are spread across multiple Systems of Record and needs to be gathered across from them.

In order to achieve uniformity of range across all the Selling Products it was decided to create a single point of entry to retrieve the range information. The Checkout-as-a-Service will create a service that will access the data from this Cache and make it be made available to the requesting channels.

This implementation of the Cache is currently done within the Selling Domain and therefore provides the same data to all accessing systems within Selling.

More details on the investigation and the solution options discussed can be found here

Write-aside Caching

!Write Aside Cache

This is similar to read-aside caching, except that instead of lazy-loading of the cache, the cached data is immediately available as the data is proactively written into the cache whenever there is new or updated information. This strategy reduces the chances of reading stale data. The complexity of implementing the caching will have to be taken care of as the read requirement has to be considered upfront so that you don't end up writing everything into the cache.

Read-through Caching

!Read through Cache

Like the read-aside caching strategy, this also loads data lazily, data is populated into the cache on the first read. The difference is that the cache is in-line with the database. The cache provider is responsible for the fetch of the data from the database unlike the read-aside.

This strategy is ideal when the same data is requested multiple times. The over-head of the first fetch in this case too can be handled by waring the cache with queries that are frequently executed. The downside if that the cache can get stale between the cache and the database.

Write-through Caching

!Write through Cache

The application writes to the cache and the cache writes into the database and when the persistence is committed the database and the cache will have consistent data.

This earns the additional overhead when persisting the data as there is two writes that accompany every persist request, but give the guarantee that the cache will have consistent data always, provided all writes are through the cache.

| Caching Strategy | Pros    | Cons    |
| :---:   | :---: | :---: |
| Read-Aside |Low write latency   |Data consistency concerns   |
|  |Reduced write load on primary data source   |Increased complexity   |
| Write-Aside |Lesser data consistency concerns   |Increased complexity   |
|  |Data available for use after the commit   |Over-head in the write logic to ensure cache is updated   |
| Read-Through |Automatic cache population on cache misses   |Data consistency concerns   |
|  |Low write latency   |Overhead of ensuring data is updated in case of updates  |
| Write-Through |Consistency can be assured   |Overhead of ensuring that all data read has writes through the cache   |
|  |Data is available immediately  |Writes are costly   |

When to Cache

* Reduce network traffic
* Content Delivery of static images, videos etc.
* Data accessed has very less changes or if proper invalidation is implemented
* Applications don't mind having stale data as against having no response
* Eventual Consistency is acceptable
* Data is accessed frequently

When Not To Cache

* Consistency is Critical
* Large Data Concepts.
* Application is mostly write intensive
* Data changes frequently
* Consistency is of higher priority than availability
* Sensitive and private data

Cache Invalidation

**Cache Expiration (TTL - Time-to-Live)**
This is based on TTL and the cache checks the validity of the data before serving it, and fetches the latest version from the source if needed. Once the TTL for a cached item elapses, the caching system automatically removes or invalidates that item from the cache.

**Manual/API Calls Cache Invalidation**
This is done to remove or update specific data or entries within a cache to ensure that the cached data remains consistent with data source and reflects the most up-to-date information. This is used to address situations where automatic cache expiration mechanisms might not be sufficient or appropriate.

**Event-Driven Cache Invalidation**
This is Push mechanism and In this case the source of the data notifies the cache through event when the data changes, and the cache updates or deletes the corresponding entry.

Go from TTL caches to infinitely caches with cache invalidation when data changes where possible.

Reference Implementations

The following list contains implementations of this pattern either in development or in production.

* IKEA.com
* Selling

Technology Platforms in scope

* In-memory caching using memcached
* Caching Platforms
* Redis

Summary

* Caching is a very useful technique offering multiple benefits for performance, scalability etc of an application
* It has be used properly with right strategy with consistency of data at core
* Consideration to be given using caching in appropriate layer in context of Ingka Retail Platform.
* Cache Invalidation should be done at right time when data in source changes
CQRS Pattern

Introduction

In this section, you will learn about the CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) pattern.

We will cover:

* What is CQRS Pattern
* The common use cases for CQRS
* How the pattern works
* What platforms will enable you apply CQRS pattern
* Where to find reference implementations
* Where to read more about CQRS

Pattern Overview

The CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) an architectural pattern that separates the responsibility for handling read and write operations on data in a system. It promotes the separation of responsibilities for handling commands (write operations) and queries (read operations) within a system, providing numerous advantages for complex software architectures.

About CQRS

CQRS, at its core, is an architectural pattern that advocates the separation of concerns between the command side and the query side of a system. It introduces a clear distinction between these two fundamental aspects of data management, offering several benefits such as independent scaling, optimized reads, and improved data security.

Use Cases

CQRS is particularly suited for scenarios where:

* The data model is complex, making it challenging to design efficient read and write operations within a single component.

* The application requires high scalability, and the read and write workloads have different scaling requirements.

* Optimized read operations are critical for delivering a responsive user experience.

* Strict auditing, logging, or access control is essential, especially in regulated industries or sensitive data environments.

Pattern Walkthrough

The CQRS pattern divides a system into two primary components:

1. Command Side: This side is responsible for processing and handling commands from clients or other parts of the system. It validates commands, updates the data store, and emits events to notify changes.

2. Query Side: The query side focuses on read operations. It retrieves data from the data store, prepares it for presentation, and serves it to clients or consumers. This side is optimized for efficient querying and may involve caching or denormalization.

The separation of these two sides allows for independent scaling, optimization, and maintenance, leading to a more robust and adaptable architecture

!CQRS Pattern

Workload Placement

We use this pattern in the following hosting locations

* Public Cloud
* On-premise

Requirements

When implementing CQRS, consider the following requirements:

* Adequate messaging and event handling infrastructure for command and event propagation.

* A well-defined data model that separates commands and queries.

* Proper event sourcing mechanisms for tracking changes to the data.

Reference Implementations

The following list contains implementations of this pattern either in development or in production.

External

* Axon Framework: A Java-based framework that simplifies CQRS and event sourcing implementations.

* EventStore: A database (Event store) designed for event sourcing and CQRS scenarios.

Internal

* SOP (Sales Order processing) a project modernising selling platform.

Technology Platforms in scope

Messaging and streaming platforms like:

* Apache Kafka
* Axonserver
* Solace(for hybrid/multi cloud scenarios)

are often used in CQRS implementations to facilitate command, Query and event handling.

More Information

For further information on CQRS and its practical implementation, consider exploring the following resources:

* CQRS Journey by Microsoft Patterns & Practices

* CQRS and Event Sourcing by Greg Young

Summary

The CQRS pattern offers a powerful solution for designing software systems that require a clear separation of command and query responsibilities. It provides scalability, performance optimization, and enhanced data security, making it valuable in a wide range of complex software scenarios. Nonetheless, it introduces challenges like eventual consistency, necessitating a certain level of team maturity and contributing to the overall complexity of its adoption.
Data Product Pattern

Introduction

In this section you will learn about the following

* What a Data Product is in IRP
* The common use cases for Data Products in IRP
* How the pattern works
* What platforms will enable you to build a Data Product fit for IRP.
* Where to find reference implementations
* Where to read more about Data Products in IRP

Pattern Overview

Data Product is an industry term that at it's core describes a particular architecture pattern for sourcing data from one or more sources, ingesting, transforming and publishing that data in a new format.

In IRP we use this pattern in a couple of different scenarios:

* To elevate data from Systems of Record to make it available in a highly scalable and resilient way, that the System of Record was not designed for.
* To combine and aggregate data from one or many sources and presenting that data as a new data concept as Business APIs.

The data flows one way only, from a data source to the consumer of the data and thus provides **read only APIs**. These APIs can be WebAPIs, Event APIs or Data Access APIs. WebAPIs provided by this patterns are good for lookup/search style scenarios, Event APIs are good for streaming data in a near realtime to subscribers, Data Access APIs are useful for Data Science, AI and ML use cases.

About Data Products

Data Products are **headless**, i.e. they do not provide a Grahical User Interface, only APIs.

A Data Product is part of the Data & Services Layer and can be considered a System of Truth in industry terms. It is often one component out of several in a system. Data Products are standardised to improve data quality, allow for data lineage and to simplify compliance.

We split reads and writes throughout the platform architecture to increase scalability. The read capability is provided by components that implement the Data Product architecture pattern.

!!! note "Information"

    If you aim to build a new system that solves both reads and writes, and will act as a System of Record, the CQRS pattern is preferred. The Data Product pattern is there as well, but done as part of the CQRS pattern.

Use Cases

* You need to get data from one or more Systems of Record, transform and publish read only APIs for use in other systems across the landscape
* You need to transform and prepare data for a Data Science use case
* You need to collect and prepare data for a dashboard
* You need to provide high performing streaming data for use in operational scenarios
* You need to provide a lookup API on data in a highly resilient, scalable way
* You need to aggregate and transform data into a new data set for publication in the Data Marketplace

Pattern Walkthrough

1. Data is sourced from one or many systems through an Input port. The method applied depends on what the source system can provide, common examples include Change Data Capture(CDC), Event Streams, Files, Datastores and APIs. The source systems may be in the same location and provider as the Data Product but can also be in a different location. When data needs to be transported between locations we recommend using technology provided for these purposes, such as Event Mesh, Private Interconnect and CDC Services. They come with compression, security and connectivity included which reduces cost and increases efficiency in data transfers.
2. Data is ingested into a raw format, stored within the boundaries of the System. This is the first level which can be used to clean, quality check, and anonymise data before further processing is done.
3. Once the data has been validated and clean it can be used un transformation and or aggregation logic to build new data entities, fit for wider consumption of other systems in the landscape.
4.

4. Data can also be derived in an additional step where more data can be added from other sources to create a new data entity.
5. Once data is formed into a new entity, it must be validated against a standardised schema, sometimes called a canonical schema. Mapping might be needed to ensure we provide the data in the agreed standardised format.
6. Data is exposed through Business APIs(Output Port), where other systems can get access to the new standardised data.
7. Datasets can be published in a Data Marketplace for Data Science use cases.

At any point above, data can be exposed and used for analytical or operational purposes. This is defined by Data Product Levels(See Data Product Reference Architecture). The use cases decides what data is needed when and by who. Throughout this process data needs to be tagged and traceable to ensure we have data lineage in place.

!Data Product

Workload Placement

We use this pattern in the following hosting locations

* Public Cloud

Requirements

See Data Product Guidelines and Standards for information on what to apply based on they type of data product you are building.

Reference Implementations

The following list contains implementations of this pattern either in development or in production.

* GRUNDLIG
* CSDF

Technology Platforms in scope

* API Management Platform
* Messaging & Streaming Platform
* Data Product Platform
* Data Storage Platform
* Data Management Platform
* Developer Platform
* Authentication & Authorisation Platform

More Information

* Data Product Reference Architecture
* Data Foundation Architecture Area

Summary

* A Data Product is an architecture pattern useful when reading data from various sources, transforming and publishing it in a standardised way across the landscape.
* A Data Product provides read-only APIs
* A Data Product is a component of a system, not a system by itself
* The Data Product pattern is a crucial part of IRP and helps to keep data standardised and compliant across the landscape
* There are common technology platforms to implement data products in a standardised way
* There are reference implementations in production today
Event Driven Architecture Pattern

Introduction

In this section you will learn about the following

* What Event Driven Architecture is
* How it supports our Architecture Strategy
* How Event Driven Architecture works in Ingka Retail Platform
* When to use this pattern and when **not** to use it.

Pattern Overview

Event Driven Architecture (EDA) is a pattern that brings together several concepts:

* Asynchronous, Message Based Communication
* Commands & Events (as coming from the Domain Driven Design community)
* Publish / Subscribe

The pattern aims to decouple systems and domains and provide a flexible architecture, in which maintainability of integrations is improved, and high availability and robustness are easier to realize.

It supports a number of EA Principles that we have defined on the corporate level (see the link for the rationale behind these principles):

* Asynchronous communication is preferred
* Decoupled Interfaces
* Build for horizontal scaling

Above aspects are also nicely explained in the Reactive Manifesto, explaining some more about the rationale.

!!! Note

    Notice that there are two variants of Event based communication that differ in typical messaging delivery guarantees:

    * Event Messaging - Here the middleware will keep track of messages consumed by subscribers and can typically support At-Least-Once delivery, where it is guaranteed that the message is delivered at least once to the consumer. Acknowledgements between consumer and middleware typically are done on a message level.

Messages that are consumed are removed at the middleware.
    * Event Streaming - Here the middleware will not by default keep track of the messages consumed, but all messages have a sequence number and are kept in the middleware. It is up to the consumer to register what sequence number was last processed successfully. When the consumer has been offline, it is up to the consumer to indicate where in the stream to restart processing. The middleware can easily facilitate replay of messages since they are not removed after consumption. At-most-once delivery is often by default offered, and configuration must be done to ensure at-least-once processing.

Use Cases

Good use cases are when:

* You want to integrate systems in a loosely coupled way
* You don't want to be dependent on the source system being available
* You don't want to be dependent on the source systems response time or scalability
* You want to have a choreography style of integration
* You want to be able to easily add more dependent systems as a subscriber without impacting the publisher
* You want to immediate react to changes happening in other parts of the landscape (without the source system needing to know about it)
* You can deal with eventual consistency across (sub)systems

Non-use cases are when:

* You have a strong dependency on getting a response directly
  * use a synchronous API instead
* You want to precisely control which systems will take what action and control this centrally
  * use Command based Orchestration instead

Pattern Walkthrough

1. An **event conveys a business relevant state transition** that has happened on the related Aggregate (and is therefore a fact).  
2. **Events are emitted when business relevant state transitions occur.**
This happens each time when an Aggregate is modified by executing a Command against it.
3. **The publisher of the Event owns the Data Concept** that is associated with the aggregate.
    1. No events are published about changes in data that is not owned by the publisher.
4. It is **guaranteed that the publishing system has also persisted the change** to the Aggregate.
5. **Multiple systems can subscribe** to an Event topic
6. **Consumer(s) and Publisher are decoupled**
    1. Location Transparent
    2. System uptime independent
    3. System response time independent
    4. Scale/throughput independent
7. **Event Driven communication facilitates Choreography** as an integration pattern (instead of Orchestration, that is Command Driven).
8.
8. **Consuming systems can cache the received event data** into their own Read Model of information that they need for processing.

Event Driven Integration

!Event Driven Integration

Event Consumption Patterns

!Event Consumption Patterns

* Private Hosting (Central/Distributed)

Requirements

* Use Event Driven Integration between Bounded Contexts to reduce coupling as much as possible fitting with the Use cases.
* A bounded context within IKEA is at least the Architecture Area. Sub contexts can exists within these areas.
* Contact your Technology Architect to help define the bounded contexts within the Architecture Area.
* Command & Events endpoints are designed for a scoped audience (e.g. a Business API to be used by other Ingka Domains). Multiple clients will reuse the same endpoints and contracts.

* Order Promise & Allocation
