* Use in scenarios where we don't require an immediate response
* Event Messaging and Event Streaming are two different ways of doing event based communication
Reference Patterns

Introduction

This section contains descriptions of different architecture patterns that fits well into the IRP architecture. They are picked based on feasability and fit-for-purpose for the intention of IRP.

While there are many architecture patterns in the industry, not all of them are suitable for us, hence guidance is needed on which ones fits, and how they should be used in IRP.

Each pattern explains what it is, how it should be used and point to real life reference implementations in our system landscape.
Interoperability Pattern

Introduction

In this section you will learn about the following

* What an interoperability layer can accomplish
* How it support uniform access to functionality
* How to design an interoperability layer
* When to use this pattern and when **not** to use it.

Pattern Overview

An interoperability layer can help in accomplishing the following:

* Common canonical data to all Consumers
* Abstracts the SaaS or COTS Systems of Record from the Consumers
* Allows for migration from one Vendor solution to another with minimal impact
* Have a standardized user experience

The interoperability pattern or the anti-corruption layer pattern is an isolation layer created to consume data or services from other systems acting as a translation layer in both directions. It is used to transform our internal models to the external models and also for making external models available for internal systems.

It supports a number of EA Principles that we have defined on the corporate level (see the link for the rationale behind these principles):

* Single source of truth
* Loosely coupled systems
* Vendor Independence
* Services are deployed independently
* Enable APIs through ReSTful and Event based APIs that follow a canonical data model

Above aspects are also nicely explained in the Reactive Manifesto, explaining some more about the rationale.

The Data and Tech Interoperability Principles that guide IKEA can be accessed here

!!!

!! Note

    The pattern typically is implemented by combining the Facade and the Adaptive Design Patterns

Use Cases

Good use cases are when:

* You want to integrate systems in a loosely coupled way
* You want to have a choreography style of integration
* You want to have the ability to change Vendors without impacting the Consumers
* You want to have the flexibility to add new Vendor software as per the Country requirements
* You want to be able to easily add more dependent systems as a subscriber without impacting the publisher
* You have a migration planned, in phases and it is required to keep the integration between current and legacy systems

Bad cases are when:

* You have significant difference in the current systems and the target systems (in case of a migration project)
* You have Vendor systems giving the information in Canonical Format and in the required communication mechanism

Pattern Walkthrough

1. The Vendor provides service(s) and/or data
2. The System Adapter layer handles the differences in the Vendor Systems by implementing the connectivity in the protocol supported
3. The data fetched from the Vendor Systems is then passed through the Processing layer
   1. Data from different sources is fetched in this layer
   2. Message is transformed from the vendor format to the required canonical format
   3. Message is then made available to the Consumers in the protocol that they communicate (Guidance on API Standards)
   4. Message is routed to the corresponding endpoint for the Consumer through Business APIs

Interoperability Patterns

!Basic Interoperability Pattern

Facade Pattern

!Facade Layer

Adapter Pattern

!Adapter Pattern

Caution

* The interoperability layer can add latency into invocations
* The layer adds overheads to the system complexity and introduces additional layers for maintenance
* The layer will have to be added to the current monitoring, logging and configuration processes
* Ensure that this layer can also be scaled

Reference Implementations

The following list contains implementations of this pattern either in development or in production.

* DSM Integration
* People Domain

Technology Platforms in scope

* Solace RDP
* API Gateway

More Information

* Microservices Architecture
* Design Patterns

Summary

* Use the interoperability layer to handle the complexities of the Business Logic and to have uniformity across all Consumers
* Do not use the layer if there is no requirement that warrants it
* Be mindful of how this layer can be scaled to meet demand
* Handle the logging and monitoring of the layer so as to enable tracing and tracking of failures
* Provide mechanisms to restart the flow from the layer that caused the failure or make provision to exit gracefully
MACH Architecture Principles and Patterns

Introduction

In this section you will learn about the following

* What is MACH Architecture
* Pros and Cons of MACH Architecture
* How are the MACH principles used in IRP
* Patterns using MACH principles
* When to use this pattern and when **not** to use it.
* Pitfalls of the MACH Architecture

Pattern Overview

MACH Architecture is an acronym that is representative of a set of architectural principles that help with making the digital landscape modular, easily testable, quickly deployable, adaptable, easy fault isolation and scalable. The principles that help in achieving this are  :

* Microservices
* API-First Design
* Cloud-Native
* Headless

MACH Architecture has gained significant acceptance in all sectors and also been shown to bring huge benefits to the Retail sector as well. The architecture has enabled get more seamless and integrated customer experiences, scale the online presence, quick time to market, improve efficiencies and optimize processes in the management of order, in fulfilment and inventory management.

It supports a number of EA Principles that we have defined on the corporate level (see the link for the rationale behind these principles):

* Single source of truth
* Loosely coupled systems
* Vendor and Development Platform Independence
* Services are deployed independently
* Enable APIs through ReSTful and Event based APIs that follow a canonical data model

Above aspects are also nicely explained in the Reactive Manifesto, explaining some more about the rationale.

Refer to the IKEA Digital Maturity Principles - Red Lines

!!! Note
MACH architecture is an acronym that stands for Microservices, API-first, Cloud-native, and Headless.
MACH architecture is commonly associated with the development of e-commerce platforms and other digital experience solutions.

Use Cases

Good use cases are when:

* You want to have a system consisting of independent deployable and versionable units.
* You want to have the ability to develop business services independent of each other
* You want to integrate systems in a loosely coupled way
* You want to have the ability to be flexible
* You want to have the ability to scale services independently depending on the need
* You want to be able to have flexibility on which cloud provider to choose
* You want to be able to drive the deployments based on configuration

Bad cases are when:

* You cannot segregate the services as independent and scalable modules
* You don't need to have a scalable or elastic application
* You don't have a distributed application with huge demands
Pattern Walkthrough

Microservices

Microservices is an architectural style where a system is composed of small, independent services that communicate with each other through well-defined APIs.

Microservices promote modularity, making it easier to develop, deploy, and scale individual components independently. This flexibility allows for faster development cycles and easier maintenance.

More on Microservices

API-First

API-First means designing and developing applications by first defining and implementing the APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) that will be used for communication between different software components.

An API-First approach ensures that different parts of a system can interact seamlessly. It encourages a focus on clear, well-documented interfaces, enabling better collaboration among development teams and facilitating integration with third-party services.

Cloud Native

Cloud-native refers to the design and operation of applications that fully leverage the advantages of cloud computing, including scalability, flexibility, and resilience.It leverages the capabilities of cloud computing to design, build, deploy and manage applications

Cloud-native applications are built and optimized for cloud environments. They can take advantage of cloud services such as storage, databases, and computing power. This principle allows for greater scalability, efficient resource utilization, and the ability to respond quickly to changing demands.

More on Cloud Native

Cloud Agnostic

Although the MACH Architecture Pattern focuses on making the systems cloud native, also look at making our solutions cloud agnostic.

Refer to Cloud Agnostic Approach

Refer to When to Choose Cloud Native or Cloud Agnostic approach for more details.

Headless Architecture

Headless architecture involves separating the front-end presentation layer (the "head") from the back-end system.

A headless approach allows for more flexibility in delivering data and functionality across different channels and devices. Data can be managed independently of the user interface, enabling a more versatile and dynamic user experience.

!Headless Architecture Pattern

Separation of Concerns: The front-end and the backend are responsible for their own functionalities and can be changed or updated independently of each other.

The front-end and the backend communicate through APIs. Decoupling the frontend and the backend gives more flexibility in what technologies can be used for the implementation of the frontend but still keep the presentation consistent.

Reference Implementations

The following list contains implementations of this pattern either in development or in production.

* DSM Integration
* People Domain

Technology Platforms in scope

* API Gateway
* Containers (like Docker)
* Orchestration Platforms (like Kubernetes)
* Serverless services (like Cloudrun)

More Information

* Microservices Architecture
* Design Patterns

Pitfalls of MACH Architecture

1. Network Latency: Communication between services usually occur over the network and this can introduce latency and complexity if not carefully handled
2. Data Management: Ensuring consistency of data in a microservices environment can be complex.
3. Service Discovery: As the number of services grows - discovering and coordinating of the services can get challenging. Implementing a service discovery mechanism is crucial and should be handled in the design
4. Version control: Versioning of the services should be handled carefully so that services can evolve independently
5. Security Concerns: Securing the communication and controlling accessibility can be a major concern
6. Testing Complexity: Testing the interactions and handling the different version can be challenging
7. Monitoring and Debugging: Monitoring and debugging can be daunting and time-consuming
8. Operational Overhead: Operating and maintaining a distributed system can have its own challenges and this needs to be handled with proper automation and management tools

Summary

* Use the principles in the MACH architecture to achieve flexibility and resilience in the systems and the services they offer
* Use the principles so as to enable the landscape to meet spurges in demand and for adapting to specific services being in demand
* Do not increase the complexity of the landscape if there is no need to use any of the layers
* Handle the monitoring and complexities that will be introduced in a distributed landscape
Multi-hybrid cloud messaging pattern

Introduction

In this section you will learn about the following

* How messages can be distributed across our hybrid-cloud system landscape
* Get an understanding of the Event Mesh
* How messages can flow from and to China

Pattern Overview

We have adopted the event mesh pattern to enable event messaging in our distributed hybrid-cloud system environment. Event Mesh is an event communication pattern designed to address the challenges of event routing, delivery, and integration in large-scale distributed systems. The event mesh plays an important role in the Ingka modernisation and Ingka become event driven.

What is the Event Mesh

The event mesh is a network of interconnected event brokers. The event mesh enables messages to travel through our distributed hybrid-cloud system landscape (On-Premises (DC7, DC8 and DC9), multi-cloud). The event mesh facilitates and accelerates the Ingka modernisation to an Event Driven Architecture EDA.

!Event Mesh Overview

Event Mesh characteristics

* Environment agnostic. It is easy to deploy in principle in any kind of environment (Docker, Kubernetes, Linux, Redhat, GCP, Azure, Ingka On-Prem standardized platforms) and has a small footprint.
* Location Transparency. Event mesh offers location transparency for publishers and subscribers. The publishers can publish events from anywhere and the subscribers can consume events anywhere.
* Dynamic message routing and filtering. The event mesh supports dynamic routing and filtering. It knows and understands dynamically what events to sent to which subscriber (consumer application) no matter where the publisher and subscriber are connected to the mesh.
* Interoperability. Event Mesh supports a wide range of messaging and event protocols, including JMS, REST, MQTT, AMQP, WebSocket, and proprietary protocols like SMF, Solace's own native protocol. This means that applications and services using different protocols can communicate with each other. Multi-protocol support allows to integrate modern, event-driven applications with legacy systems. It promotes interoperability between systems built on different technologies.
* Smart WAN fanout.

A single message can have multiple subscribers across the WAN link, Event Mesh can send it just once over the WAN link, and fan it out via copy of that many times at the remote location. This efficient approach minimizes the duplication of the same event data over the network, optimizing bandwidth usage and efficient routing reduce the latency experienced by subscribers, ensuring that events are delivered as quickly as possible.

Global and China Event Mesh

The Ingka event mesh is actually not one big event mesh of interconnected brokers. It consists of a Global mesh and the China Mesh. The brokers in the Global and China mesh are connected via dynamic links internally within each mesh. This means that messages flow automatically within each mesh, but not between. The Global and China Mesh are connected via a static link, which enables us to control what data flows between the meshes. This was a conscious design decision to make sure we can comply to the China compliancy rules and control what data flows to and from China to Global. The static link must be configured for data to flow between the Global and China Mesh.

Use Cases

Good use cases are when:

* Topic based Publish & Subscribe model
* Asynchronous messaging pattern
* Event based communication
* System wants to publish a business event that is of interest to one or more other systems
* Subscribing systems need to filter on the business event topic
* Subscribing systems are deployed in the China Data center or AliCloud
* System needs guaranteed delivery globally, irrespective of hosting location, region or system architecture

Bad use cases are when:

* You have a strong dependency on getting a response directly (use a sync API instead)
* Private Events between system components
* Event Streaming where messages are kept in the middleware

Pattern Walkthrough

In a topic based publish-subscribe messaging model, the publisher define a topic and publish events to this topic at runtime (logical channel). One or more Subscribers subscribe to the topic and will thereafter receive all the events published to that topic.

!Publish & Subscribe

The event mesh distributes event messages published by a publisher on the event mesh to the subscribers. One of the main advantages of the topic publish-subscribe model is the decoupling or loose coupling of the publishers and subscribers. The publisher don't know and do not have to know about the subscribers. It is the topic that decouples the subscriber from the publisher.

Design-time

1. Publishing system (Publisher) defines event topic according to the AsyncAPI standard

2. Publisher publishes event topic in the event catalog

3. Subscriber discovers event topic in the API Portal in Allen developer portal

4. Subscriber subscribes to event topic

Run-time

1. An event, business relevant state transition, occurs in the publishing system

2. Publishing system publishes the event on the event mesh

3. Systems that have subscribed to the event receive the event

Workload Placement

We use this pattern in the following hosting locations

* Public Cloud
  * GCP
  * AliCloud
  * Azure
* Private Hosting (Central/Distributed)

Requirements

* Use Event Driven Integration between Bounded Contexts to reduce coupling as much as possible fitting with the Use cases. A bounded context within IKEA is at least the Architecture Area. Sub contexts can exists within these areas.

Reference Implementations

* Event Messaging

Sample Implementation

* Work Order Manager (WOMGR)
* Store Application Gateway (SAG)
* Sales Location Management (SLM)

More Information

Replay of data across systems

Introduction

In this section you will learn about the following

* How we replay data across the system landscape
* The uses cases where this pattern is applicable
* How this pattern can be implemented

Pattern Overview

Event messaging in EDA does not decouple the publisher and subscribers entirely from each other. It does in the happy flow, but not in the unhappy flow. There are several problem scenarios that can occur on either publisher or subscriber side that require event resend or replay. Publishers and subscribers make assumptions during the design on the capabilities of each other and the event mesh, like for example:

* Publisher assumes that the subscriber can handle duplicates
* Subscriber assumes that they will not get duplicates.
* Subscriber assumes that the publisher can replay events from a certain moment in time to a specific subscriber
* Subscriber assumes that the publisher can resend specific events on demand to them only
* Subscribers and/or publishers assume that the event mesh can resend or replay any message from a specific point in time

To overcome these challenges, this self service Event Replay pattern can help.

Use Cases

* The subscriber has dropped one, more or all events and requests the publisher to
  * resend or replay events from a certain moment in time
  * resend certain specific events
  * perform a complete resend.  This is probably an exceptional cases e.g. data corruption.
* New subscriber requiring a full baseload of
  * the latest actual data, for example master data
  * all data including historical data

Pattern Walkthrough

To create a robust event distribution through our system landscape must we clarify the responsibilities of the publisher and subscriber in the publish and subscribe pattern. The decouple the publishers and subscribers as much as possible shall it be possible for subscribers to recover from a down stream error themselves.

In other words, the subscribers shall be able to resend and replay messages from any publisher without the help of the publisher:

* subscriber must be able to resend objects to itself
* subscriber must be able to replay messages from a specific point in time to itself
* subscriber must be able handle duplicate messages (idempotent)
* subscriber must be able to consume resend and replay messages in the same way/flow as the original events or in a temporary topic/queue defined by the subscriber
* publisher and messaging & streaming platform must guarantee at least once delivery
* publisher must enable subscribers to resend objects to themselves
* publisher must enable subscribers to replay messages from a specific point in time to themselves
* publisher must enable subscribers to replay messages by key to themselves
* publisher must be able to provide new subscribers with a full historical or actual data baseload
* publisher must preserve a persistent event store with all historical data

!!! Note

    Resend and replay messages in the same way/flow as the original events or in a temporary topic/queue defined by the subscriber is an important design consideration.

Replay in the same way/flow as the original events implies that all the subscribers will receive the messages although they haven't requested the replay. Although the subsribers must be able to handle duplicate messages does it put an unnessecary load on the sbscribers especially in the case of a full historical or actual data load.
    Inter/Ingka Resend/Replay Principles doesn't support resend/replay between companies. The subscribing company has the obligation to store all received messages. The receiving company needs to store all messages in an event store from where “internal” resending can be done.

We use this pattern in the following hosting locations

* Public Cloud
  * GCP
  * AliCloud
  * Azure
* On-premises (DC7/8, DC9)

Requirements

Event publishers must provide subscribers with an API that enables the subscribers to resend and replay messages themselves whenever needed.

Summary

* Replay needs to be standardised across systems to ensure data quality
* Publishers must provide an API that enables subscribers to replay/resend data
* We have agreed data distribution principles on this topic with Inter-IKEA
* This pattern can span hosting locations, to enable replay between cloud and on-prem, or between cloud providers

What is the Ingka Retail Platform?

> “The diversity of the literature on platforms raises the question of whether the various “platform” concepts are synonymous or simply evocative uses of the same word to mean different things. We subscribe to the former view, arguing that at the level of architecture all platform systems are fundamentally the same. On this view, **a platform architecture displays a special type of modularity, in which a product or system is split into a set of components with low variety and high reusability, and another set with high variety and low reusability**. The first set is called “the platform.” The second has no generic name but might be called “the complements” of the platform. The platform and its complements are distinct modules in the system architecture. Their interoperability is made possible via design rules, that is, interface specifications binding on both” *Source: HBS EDU

Establishing an Ingka Retail Platform in the tech landscape means it offer capabilities and features that drive experiences. The aim is to enable that we can build experiences on top of reusing business APIs.

They stretch across the complete customer journey from inspiration through the buying process to after sales and backward flows. The APIs expose the key data objects used in the enterprise and are independent of the the products' implementation. These **standard enterprise data objects has its own lifecycle** and always expose through Web API, Event API and Datasets (Data Access APIs) designed to consume for analytics and operational/transactional use cases managing large datasets. The API are designed for High availability and High Partition Tolerence (referring to CAP Theorem) which also means we need to decouple our System of Records that has never been designed for managing this type of scale and performance.

Below is the walkthrough of the layers in this platform architecture which is the architecture for the digital foundation we need to establish. This is what we mean by saying Retail Business Platform.

Experience ecosystem

Characteristics of the experience ecosystem:

* Closest to the business user (Customer, Coworker etc.)
* Serve the same business client(s) supporting a distinct experience.
* **Low cohesion, high variability, and low reusability**
* Require working together on data that needs to be absolutely consistent at any point in time
* **This layer does not directly change the state of the core data**
  * The experience layer publishes events that will be ingested by the Core systems to make the necessary core data changes.
* All core data is accessed through the business platform surface

Business APIs & Services (Systems of truth)

The core business capabilities of our enterprise (Business Process, logic, data, insights, and decisions) are exposed to the Enterprise (Internal systems and our partner ecosystem) as business apis & services

Characteristics of the Business APIs & Services:

* Business view APIs that expose the Core data
  * Performance & resilience are achieved by materializing standardized data streams from the core into read only views for API, reactive, and analytic query patterns.
* Business logic APIs
  * The business logic that runs a context specific algorithm to compute an outcome. For example - Tax & fee, Price & Promotion etc.
* Business process APIs
  * The process that facilitates the process steps to achieve an outcome. For example – Checkout, Cart, Fulfillment etc.
* **With respect to CAP theorem these are AVAILABLE & PARTITION TOLERANT systems satisfying the A&P in CAP.**
  * To encourage horizontal scale High Availability is the primary concern here.

Core data systems (Systems of record)

A set of fundamental data that is core to the various domains of the Enterprise. Characteristics of the core data system:

* These are owned, managed, and can only be created or changed by their respective domains/products.
  * *For example – Product, Price, Inventory, Location, Customer, Coworker, Partners etc.
* **With respect to CAP theorem these are CONSISTENT & AVAILABLE systems satisfying the C&A in CAP.**
  * Since it is the system of record and needs to capture the state change to reflect the real time state of an entity CONSISTENCY is the primary concern here.

Insights & Decision ecosystem (Systems of Insights)

An integrated data platform that supports and enables the domain level values for IKEA operational intelligence and Data science

This is leveraged by the business for:

* operational reporting,
* mature IKEA customer models to drive personalization etc.
With respect to CAP theorem **these are AVAILABLE & PARTITION TOLERANT systems satisfying the A&P in CAP**.

Catalogues

Introduction

In this section you will learn more about

* What catalogues to we have?
* What do we use each one for?
* Where do I find each catalogue?

Enterprise Catalogues

In our landscape we manage a wide range of systems, components, APIs, tools and schemas that enables us to discover and reuse assets. We also need to know what assets we have as a company, so we understand what is affected by changes. The main catalogues important to know about is listed in this document.

Data Catalogue

The Data Catalogue contains all our Data Concepts. Data Concepts is a high level defintion of data we need to manage as a company. In IKEA we manage everything from customers and orders to suppliers and stores, and each of these have a data concept that represnts it in the digital world. The Data Catalogue and Data Concepts enables us to use a common vocabulary across the organisation, and also what the most important attributes are for each of the data concepts. By harmonising this, we will avoid variants across the system landscape and reduce the risk of duplications and mapping issues.

We link Business APIs to Data Concepts to ensure we know what Data Concepts we implement, use and expose in an API.

The Data Catalogue can be found at https://datacatalog.ingka.com

System Master Catalogue

The Sytem Master Catalogue, SMC, keeps track of all our systems. It is the master for all Systems in the landscape, and describes them using different attributes. It helps us to establish ownership, both organisationally and architecturally, and contains useful information about systems.

All systems needs to be registered in SMC, in order for it to be visible in different places across the landscape. It is the source for both strategic plans and for tooling.

The System Master Catalogue can be found at https://jira.digital.ingka.com/secure/ObjectSchema.jspa?id=17

API Catalogue

The API catalogue manages all our Business APIs and is an internal API Catalogue. This means it's primarily focused on providing insights for Ingka co-workers and not external partners. It keeps track of what APIs we have, who owns them, what the specification is, what versions are available and more.  This is useful to discover APIs, generate code stubs and to learn about what APIs are available to build new software on.

In IRP, this catalogue contains the Business APIs that expose the platform functionality.

The API Catalogue can be found at https://allen.ingka.com/api-docs

Component Catalogue

The Component Catalogue contains the software components we have in our landscape. This helps discovery of shared components that can be reused, and helps you discover code across the landscape. These components makes up the Systems we manage in System Master Catalogue and is thus a level down in detail from what SMC gives.

By registering components in the catalogue we increase transparency and discoverability, and contributes to building organisational knowledge about our landscape.

The Component Catalogue can be found in Allen
